choices.text: Because she is asymptomatic, no special cervical-spine precautions are needed and standard direct laryngoscopy positioning is appropriate
choices.rationale: Up to ~15–30% of children with Down syndrome have asymptomatic atlantoaxial laxity, so the absence of symptoms does not eliminate the need for neck-protective positioning.

explanation: Children with Down syndrome have ligamentous laxity at the atlanto-occipital and atlantoaxial joints, and a substantial minority have asymptomatic radiographic instability that can be missed by symptom history alone. Routine preoperative flexion–extension radiographs are no longer recommended for screening because they do not reliably predict neurologic injury; instead, every child with trisomy 21 should be treated as potentially unstable. Maintaining a neutral cervical spine and using a video laryngoscope to minimize the head extension required for intubation reduces the risk of C1–C2 subluxation and spinal cord injury.
subtopic: Pediatric syndromic airway — Down syndrome and atlantoaxial instability

verdict.reason: CLINICAL: Keyed answer (index 1) is the single best answer. For an asymptomatic Down syndrome child with no recent imaging, current standard is to treat the cervical spine as potentially unstable: maintain a neutral neck, minimize cervical manipulation, and use video laryngoscopy to reduce required head extension. All four distractors are genuinely inferior: routine flexion-extension screening films are no longer recommended (poor predictive value for cord injury); maximal extension is wrong because instability is dynamic and extension can also cause C1-C2 subluxation; deep axial cervical traction risks cord compression and is contraindicated; and "asymptomatic = no precautions" is wrong because ~15-30% have asymptomatic radiographic atlantoaxial laxity. Rationales and the explanation are accurate and current (consistent with AAP guidance against routine radiographic screening). REFERENCE: WebFetch of https://www.openanesthesia.org/keywords/down-syndrome/ resolves and is directly on-topic — it explicitly states joint laxity at atlantoaxial/atlantooccipital joints with up to ~30% having radiographic instability, that routine preoperative C-spine X-rays are not recommended for asymptomatic patients, and that VL with minimized head extension is preferred. Both clinical content and citation are sound.

stem: A 71-year-old man is scheduled for an open right hemicolectomy for colon cancer. His history includes a non-ST-elevation myocardial infarction with a drug-eluting stent placed 18 months ago, insulin-dependent type 2 diabetes, and chronic kidney disease with a serum creatinine of 2.4 mg/dL. He has no anginal symptoms and reports that he can climb two flights of stairs to his bedroom and carry groceries without stopping or becoming short of breath. His resting ECG shows old Q waves inferiorly but no acute changes. Based on the Revised Cardiac Risk Index and his functional capacity, which of the following is the most appropriate next step in his preoperative cardiac evaluation?

choices.text: Cancel surgery until coronary angiography and possible revascularization are completed
choices.rationale: Prophylactic revascularization before noncardiac surgery does not improve outcomes in stable patients and is not indicated in an asymptomatic patient with adequate functional capacity.

choices.text: Order a dobutamine stress echocardiogram to quantify ischemic burden
choices.rationale: Stress testing is reserved for patients with poor or unknown functional capacity (less than 4 METs) when it would change management, neither of which applies here.

choices.text: Order a resting transthoracic echocardiogram to estimate ejection fraction
choices.rationale: Routine resting echocardiography is not indicated solely for risk stratification in an asymptomatic patient without signs of heart failure or valvular disease.

choices.text: Proceed to surgery without further cardiac testing because his functional capacity is adequate
choices.rationale: Despite an elevated RCRI, his ability to perform greater than 4 METs of activity without symptoms means further noninvasive testing is unlikely to change management, so he should proceed.

choices.text: Initiate high-dose metoprolol the morning of surgery to blunt perioperative ischemia
choices.rationale: Starting high-dose beta-blockade on the day of surgery in a beta-blocker-naive patient increases stroke and mortality risk and is contraindicated.

explanation: This patient has at least three RCRI predictors (coronary heart disease, insulin-dependent diabetes, and creatinine >2 mg/dL), plus high-risk intraperitoneal surgery, placing him in an elevated-risk category. However, the ACC/AHA stepwise algorithm pivots on functional capacity: a patient who can perform greater than or equal to 4 METs (e.g., climbing two flights of stairs) without symptoms can proceed to surgery, because further noninvasive testing rarely changes management or improves outcomes. Stress testing is reserved for poor or indeterminate functional capacity when results would alter the plan, and acute high-dose beta-blocker initiation on the day of surgery is harmful.
subtopic: Preoperative cardiac risk stratification (RCRI and functional capacity)
reference_title: Cardiac Risk Assessment - StatPearls - NCBI Bookshelf

verdict.reason: CLINICAL: Keyed answer (index 3, "Proceed to surgery without further cardiac testing") is the single best answer and is unambiguous. The patient has 4 RCRI predictors (ischemic heart disease s/p NSTEMI/DES, insulin-dependent diabetes, creatinine 2.4 >2.0 mg/dL, and high-risk intraperitoneal surgery), so risk is elevated — but per the 2014 ACC/AHA stepwise algorithm, a stable patient with no active cardiac condition and adequate functional capacity (>=4 METs, here climbing 2 flights / carrying groceries) proceeds to surgery because noninvasive testing rarely changes management. The explanation's "at least three RCRI predictors" is accurate (conservative; truly 4). The DES was placed 18 months ago, well past the recommended 6-12 month elective-surgery delay window, so stent timing reinforces rather than contradicts "proceed" — no hidden trap. All distractors are genuinely inferior with accurate rationales: A (prophylactic revascularization not beneficial in stable patients, per CARP/DECREASE-V); B (stress testing reserved for poor/unknown functional capacity when it would change management); C (routine resting echo not indicated absent HF/valvular signs or dyspnea — patient has neither and good functional capacity); E (acute high-dose day-of beta-blockade in a naive patient is harmful per POISE, increasing stroke/death). REFERENCE: WebFetch of https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK537146/ resolves to the StatPearls "Cardiac Risk Assessment" article, which directly covers RCRI (including creatinine >2 mg/dL and insulin-dependent diabetes as predictors), METs/functional capacity, the ACC/AHA stepwise algorithm, and POISE-based beta-blockade caution — fully on-topic and matching the citation title. Both clinical and reference are sound.

stem: A 68-year-old man with a 6.2 cm infrarenal abdominal aortic aneurysm is undergoing open repair under combined general and epidural anesthesia. He has hypertension and a 50-pack-year smoking history with mild COPD. Just before graft anastomosis, the surgeon applies an infrarenal aortic cross-clamp. The anesthesiologist anticipates the hemodynamic consequences. Compared with a supraceliac clamp, which of the following best describes the expected hemodynamic effect of this infrarenal clamp placement?

choices.text: A marked rise in preload as blood volume shifts from below the clamp into the central circulation
choices.rationale: A pronounced preload increase is characteristic of a supraceliac clamp, not an infrarenal one, because the splanchnic bed lies above an infrarenal clamp and is not excluded from the circulation.

choices.text: Little to no rise in preload because the splanchnic venous bed absorbs the redistributed blood volume
choices.rationale: With an infrarenal clamp the patent splanchnic circulation accommodates redistributed blood, so central preload typically does not increase substantially.

choices.text: A fall in afterload due to reflex vasodilation distal to the clamp
choices.rationale: Afterload rises rather than falls with aortic clamping because of the abrupt increase in impedance to aortic outflow.

choices.text: A profound drop in coronary perfusion pressure and immediate myocardial ischemia
choices.rationale: Infrarenal clamping generally produces minimal hemodynamic derangement and is far less likely to precipitate ischemia than a supraceliac clamp.

choices.text: An immediate severe metabolic alkalosis from accumulation of ischemic metabolites
choices.rationale: Tissue ischemia distal to the clamp produces lactic acidosis, not alkalosis, and this develops over time rather than immediately.

explanation: The hemodynamic impact of aortic cross-clamping depends heavily on clamp level. A supraceliac clamp excludes the splanchnic venous reservoir, so blood is forced into the central circulation and preload (and afterload) rises sharply. With an infrarenal clamp, the splanchnic bed remains in continuity above the clamp and absorbs redistributed volume, so preload typically does not increase appreciably and overall hemodynamic changes are modest. Afterload still increases because of the rise in aortic input impedance, and ischemia distal to the clamp causes lactic acidosis rather than alkalosis.
subtopic: Aortic cross-clamp physiology (clamp level and preload)

verdict.reason: CLINICAL: The keyed answer (index 1) is the single best answer and reflects the canonical Gelman/Miller framework on aortic cross-clamp physiology. The hemodynamic impact depends on clamp level relative to the splanchnic venous reservoir. With an infrarenal clamp the splanchnic bed lies ABOVE the clamp and remains in continuity with the central circulation, so it accommodates redistributed blood volume and central preload does not rise appreciably—hemodynamic changes are modest. This contrasts with a supraceliac clamp, which excludes the splanchnic reservoir and forces blood centrally, sharply raising preload and afterload. All four distractors are genuinely inferior with accurate rationales: A describes the supraceliac (not infrarenal) response; C is wrong because afterload RISES (increased aortic input impedance) rather than falls; D overstates ischemia risk for an infrarenal clamp; E is wrong because distal ischemia causes lactic ACIDOSIS, not alkalosis. The explanation is accurate and current. The qualifier 'little to no rise...typically' appropriately hedges the known sympathetic-tone dependence, and the stem's explicit framing 'Compared with a supraceliac clamp' makes the answer unambiguous. REFERENCE: WebFetch of https://www.openanesthesia.org/keywords/aortic-cross-clamping/ resolves successfully; the page is authored by Aaron Sachs MD and directly covers clamp-level-dependent blood volume redistribution, the splanchnic reservoir's role, increased afterload/myocardial oxygen demand, and ischemia-reperfusion/metabolic acidosis—squarely on-topic. Both clinical and reference checks pass.

stem: A 9-year-old child with myelomeningocele (spina bifida) and a neurogenic bladder presents for revision of a ventriculoperitoneal shunt. He has had numerous prior urologic and neurosurgical procedures. About 40 minutes into an uneventful general anesthetic, after the surgeon has been working in the wound with gloved hands, the patient develops diffuse urticaria, progressive bronchospasm with rising peak airway pressures, and hypotension with a blood pressure of 62/30 mm Hg. No new intravenous drug has been administered in the preceding 30 minutes. Which of the following is the most likely trigger of this reaction, and what is the most important measure to prevent it in such patients?

choices.text: Residual neuromuscular blocker; pretreat with a histamine-2 antagonist
choices.rationale: Neuromuscular blockers are given at induction and would not newly trigger anaphylaxis 40 minutes later with no recent dosing, and H2 blockers do not reliably prevent anaphylaxis.

choices.text: Chlorhexidine skin prep; substitute povidone-iodine for all skin antisepsis
choices.rationale: Chlorhexidine can cause anaphylaxis, but the delayed onset coinciding with surgical glove contact and this high-risk population points more strongly to latex than to a prep applied at the start of the case.

choices.text: Latex protein from surgical gloves; manage the patient in a latex-free environment from the outset
choices.rationale: Children with spina bifida and multiple prior surgeries have a very high prevalence of latex sensitization, and delayed intraoperative anaphylaxis coinciding with glove contact is the classic presentation, making a latex-free environment the key preventive measure.

choices.text: The volatile anesthetic agent; convert to a total intravenous anesthetic technique
choices.rationale: Volatile anesthetics are exceedingly rare causes of true anaphylaxis and would not explain a reaction beginning well after a steady-state inhalational anesthetic was established.

choices.text: Transfusion-related reaction; stop any blood products and send a transfusion workup
choices.rationale: No transfusion is described in this scenario, so a transfusion reaction cannot explain the findings.

explanation: Children with spina bifida who have undergone multiple operations have among the highest rates of latex sensitization of any group (reported up to roughly 70%), making latex a leading cause of intraoperative anaphylaxis in this population. The hallmark is a delayed-onset reaction (often 30 to 60 minutes into the case) that coincides with mucosal or wound contact with latex gloves rather than with a bolus drug, distinguishing it from drug anaphylaxis at induction. The single most important preventive strategy is to manage these patients in a latex-free environment from the start of care, not to react after sensitization occurs.
subtopic: Latex allergy and perioperative anaphylaxis risk

verdict.reason: CLINICAL: Keyed answer (index 2, latex from surgical gloves; latex-free environment from the outset) is the single best answer. The stem is a textbook latex anaphylaxis vignette: a spina bifida/myelomeningocele child with multiple prior surgeries (highest-risk sensitization group, ~20-65% up to ~70%), delayed onset ~40 min coinciding with the surgeon's gloved-hand wound contact, urticaria + bronchospasm with rising peak pressures + hypotension, and explicit exclusion of any recent IV drug. All distractors are genuinely inferior: residual NMB is given at induction and would not newly trigger 40 min later (and H2 blockers don't prevent anaphylaxis); volatile agents are exceedingly rare anaphylaxis causes at steady state; no transfusion is described. Chlorhexidine is the strongest distractor and a real perioperative anaphylaxis cause, but prep is applied at case start, and the timing/population point decisively to latex — it is correctly subordinated, not co-equal. Explanation is accurate and current (latex is a leading cause of intraoperative anaphylaxis; latex-free environment from the start is the key preventive measure). REFERENCE: WebFetch of https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK545164/ resolves; title confirmed as 'Latex Allergy - StatPearls - NCBI Bookshelf'; page explicitly covers latex anaphylaxis, spina bifida as a risk factor, latex as a top intraoperative anaphylaxis cause, and latex-free management — directly on-topic.

stem: A 71-year-old man with a 90% symptomatic left internal carotid stenosis undergoes left carotid endarterectomy under general anesthesia. His usual blood pressure is 158/88 mmHg, and he takes amlodipine and atorvastatin. As the surgeon places the carotid cross-clamp, the stump pressure is measured at 38 mmHg and the EEG shows new ipsilateral slowing. The current MAP is 78 mmHg and end-tidal CO2 is 30 mmHg. Which intervention is most appropriate to optimize cerebral perfusion during cross-clamping?

choices.text: Raise the MAP with phenylephrine to roughly 20% above the patient's baseline and allow the end-tidal CO2 to rise toward normocapnia
choices.rationale: Correct: augmenting MAP about 20% above baseline maximizes collateral flow through the circle of Willis, and restoring normocapnia avoids hypocapnic vasoconstriction that worsens the ischemic deficit.

choices.text: Deliberately hyperventilate the patient to an end-tidal CO2 of 25 mmHg to reduce cerebral metabolic demand
choices.rationale: Incorrect: hypocapnia causes cerebral vasoconstriction and a 'steal' that further reduces collateral flow to the at-risk hemisphere, worsening ischemia.

choices.text: Lower the MAP toward 60 mmHg to reduce the risk of embolization from the plaque
choices.rationale: Incorrect: hypotension during cross-clamping critically reduces collateral perfusion pressure and is the opposite of what the stump pressure and EEG changes call for.

choices.text: Administer mannitol 0.5 g/kg to lower intracranial pressure and improve cerebral perfusion pressure
choices.rationale: Incorrect: ICP is not elevated in this setting, and mannitol does nothing to address the inadequate collateral perfusion pressure produced by the cross-clamp.

choices.text: Deepen the anesthetic with a propofol bolus to achieve burst suppression as the sole neuroprotective measure
choices.rationale: Incorrect: while burst suppression lowers metabolic rate, giving a propofol bolus without first correcting MAP would drop perfusion pressure and is not the primary maneuver.

explanation: During carotid cross-clamping, perfusion to the ipsilateral hemisphere depends on collateral flow across the circle of Willis, which is pressure-dependent. Standard practice is to maintain MAP at least 20% above the patient's preoperative baseline, particularly when stump pressure is low (often <40-50 mmHg) or EEG shows ischemia, and to target normocapnia because hypocapnia causes vasoconstriction that reduces cerebral blood flow. Hyperventilation and induced hypotension both aggravate the ischemic deficit.
subtopic: Carotid endarterectomy: cerebral perfusion and blood pressure management during cross-clamping

verdict.reason:
CLINICAL: Sound and unambiguous. With a low stump pressure (38 mmHg, below the typical <40-50 ischemic threshold), new ipsilateral EEG slowing, and a current MAP (78) well below the patient's baseline (158/88, MAP ~111), the keyed answer—augment MAP with phenylephrine to ~20% above baseline plus restore normocapnia—is the single best maneuver. Collateral flow across the circle of Willis during cross-clamping is pressure-dependent, and hypocapnia (ETCO2 30) causes vasoconstriction that worsens the ischemic deficit, so allowing CO2 to rise toward normocapnia is correct. All distractors are genuinely inferior: hyperventilation to 25 mmHg (B) worsens ischemia via vasoconstriction; lowering MAP to 60 (C) reduces collateral perfusion pressure; mannitol (D) addresses a non-existent ICP problem; and a propofol bolus to burst suppression "as the sole measure / without first correcting MAP" (E) would drop perfusion pressure and is not the primary intervention—the qualifiers make it clearly inferior to A. Rationales and explanation are accurate and current.

REFERENCE: WebFetched successfully (HTTP 200). The StatPearls "Carotid Endarterectomy" page (NBK470582) is real and genuinely on-topic for the surgical procedure. It does not contain the specific intraoperative MAP/stump-pressure/CO2 details, but it is the correct procedure and a legitimate, on-topic citation—not fabricated, 404, or mismatched. Minor only because the citation is topical rather than pinpoint to the perfusion-management teaching point.

stem: A 54-year-old woman is undergoing a posterior fossa craniotomy for resection of an acoustic neuroma in the sitting position under general anesthesia with an air/oxygen/sevoflurane technique. Roughly 40 minutes into the resection, the anesthesiologist notes an abrupt drop in end-tidal CO2 from 34 to 19 mmHg, a 'mill-wheel' murmur on the precordial Doppler, a falling SpO2, and hypotension. Which combination of actions represents the most appropriate immediate management?

choices.text: Increase the inspired oxygen toward 100%, leave the surgical field open to room air, and lower the head further to increase venous return
choices.rationale: Incorrect: although 100% oxygen is correct, leaving the field exposed and lowering the head would worsen air entrainment because the wound remains above the heart.

choices.text: Switch to 100% oxygen, notify the surgeon to flood the field with saline and apply bone wax, attempt aspiration through a central catheter, and provide hemodynamic support
choices.rationale: Correct: this bundle stops further air entry by occluding the open veins, increases the gradient for nitrogen washout, evacuates entrained air, and supports the circulation, which is the standard response to hemodynamically significant venous air embolism.

choices.text: Begin rapid hyperventilation to an end-tidal CO2 of 20 mmHg to compensate for the dead-space increase
choices.rationale: Incorrect: hyperventilation does not address the air lock and the hypocapnia here reflects pulmonary dead space from the embolism, not inadequate ventilation that needs correcting.

choices.text: Administer a 20% lipid emulsion bolus over 2 to 3 minutes to bind the circulating air
choices.rationale: Incorrect: lipid emulsion is the treatment for local anesthetic systemic toxicity and has no role in clearing intravascular air.

choices.text: Immediately initiate chest compressions and call for cardiopulmonary bypass before any other maneuver
choices.rationale: Incorrect: the patient has a perfusing rhythm with hypotension, so compressions and bypass are premature before standard air-embolism measures are attempted.

explanation: A sudden fall in end-tidal CO2 with a mill-wheel murmur, desaturation, and hypotension during a sitting craniotomy is classic for venous air embolism, which occurs when non-collapsible dural sinuses are open above the level of the heart. Immediate management includes 100% oxygen (and stopping nitrous oxide if used), notifying the surgeon to flood/pack the field and identify the entry point, aspirating air through a right-atrial catheter, providing vasopressor/inotropic support, and lowering the head only to bring the wound below heart level. Hyperventilation and lipid emulsion do not treat the embolus.
subtopic: Venous air embolism during sitting-position posterior fossa craniotomy

verdict.reason: CLINICAL: The stem is a textbook presentation of hemodynamically significant venous air embolism during sitting-position posterior fossa craniotomy (abrupt EtCO2 drop 34->19, mill-wheel murmur on precordial Doppler, desaturation, hypotension). Keyed answer B (100% O2 + stop air entry by flooding field/bone wax + aspirate via central/RA catheter + hemodynamic support) is the single best answer and matches the standard response. All distractors are genuinely inferior: A correctly uses 100% O2 but leaving the wound open and the muddled positioning worsens entrainment; C (hyperventilation) does not address the air lock and misreads the dead-space hypocapnia; D (lipid emulsion) is for LAST, no role for intravascular air; E (compressions/CPB first) is premature with a perfusing rhythm. The explanation is accurate and current. Only minor nuance: B's rationale invokes 'nitrogen washout' as a mechanism for 100% O2, which is a real but secondary effect (the primary reasons are treating hypoxemia and avoiding N2O bubble expansion) - defensible and does not affect the key. REFERENCE: WebFetch of https://www.openanesthesia.org/keywords/venous-air-embolism/ resolves to a legitimate, current (updated March 2026) OpenAnesthesia article authored by Mayo Clinic physicians that covers exactly the diagnostic signs (mill-wheel murmur, EtCO2 drop, precordial Doppler/TEE) and management (notify surgeon to flood field/bone wax, stop N2O + 100% O2, RA catheter aspiration, vasopressor/inotrope support) in this question. On-topic and accurate.

stem: A 48-year-old man undergoes endoscopic transsphenoidal resection of a nonfunctioning pituitary macroadenoma. He is extubated uneventfully and transferred to the ICU. Beginning about 8 hours postoperatively, he produces 380-420 mL/hr of dilute urine for three consecutive hours. Repeat labs show serum sodium 149 mEq/L (rising from 141), serum osmolality 302 mOsm/kg, and urine osmolality 110 mOsm/kg. He is awake and reports intense thirst. Which intervention is most appropriate?

choices.text: Restrict free water and observe, attributing the polyuria to mobilization of intraoperative fluids
choices.rationale: Incorrect: the rising sodium, high serum osmolality, and inappropriately dilute urine indicate true central diabetes insipidus, not a benign postoperative diuresis, and fluid restriction would worsen hypernatremia.

choices.text: Begin a hypertonic (3%) saline infusion to correct the hypernatremia
choices.rationale: Incorrect: hypertonic saline would add to the sodium load and worsen hypernatremia; the problem is free-water loss, not sodium deficit.

choices.text: Start a continuous furosemide infusion to manage the high urine output
choices.rationale: Incorrect: furosemide increases free-water loss and would aggravate the hypernatremia and volume depletion.

choices.text: Administer desmopressin (DDAVP) and replace the free-water deficit while monitoring serum sodium and urine output closely
choices.rationale: Correct: the picture of polyuria with rising serum sodium, high serum osmolality, and inappropriately low urine osmolality after pituitary surgery is central diabetes insipidus, treated with DDAVP plus careful free-water replacement and serial sodium monitoring.

choices.text: Hold all therapy and recheck labs in 12 hours, since transient diabetes insipidus usually resolves spontaneously
choices.rationale: Incorrect: with sodium already 149 mEq/L and ongoing massive dilute urine output, delaying treatment risks progressive hypernatremia and hypovolemia and is unsafe.

explanation: Central diabetes insipidus is a recognized complication after transsphenoidal pituitary surgery, caused by injury to the hypothalamic-neurohypophyseal axis and deficient ADH release. It presents with polyuria (often >300 mL/hr), dilute urine (urine osmolality low relative to a high serum osmolality), and rising serum sodium. Management is desmopressin together with replacement of the free-water deficit and close monitoring of serum sodium, because both undertreatment (hypernatremia) and overtreatment (iatrogenic hyponatremia, which can mimic the SIADH seen later in the postoperative course) are dangerous.
subtopic: Transsphenoidal hypophysectomy: postoperative central diabetes insipidus and DDAVP management

verdict.reason: Clinical content is correct and unambiguous. The vignette is classic post-transsphenoidal central DI: polyuria (~380-420 mL/hr, projecting >9 L/day), inappropriately dilute urine (osm 110) against a high serum osm (302) and rising hypernatremia (141->149). DDAVP plus free-water deficit replacement with serial sodium monitoring (option D) is the single best answer and standard management. All distractors are genuinely inferior: fluid restriction (A), 3% saline (B), and furosemide (C) each worsen the free-water deficit/hypernatremia, and delaying therapy 12h (E) is unsafe with Na already 149 and rising. Although mild DI in an awake patient with intact thirst can sometimes be managed by oral water alone, the magnitude here (output well above the ~4-6 L/day conservative threshold and Na trending up) makes DDAVP appropriate, not premature, so there is no genuine second defensible answer. The explanation's mention of later SIADH/triphasic response and the dual danger of under- and over-correction is accurate. REFERENCE: WebFetch of https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK556142/ resolves to the StatPearls 'Transsphenoidal Hypophysectomy' article, which directly covers postoperative diabetes insipidus (cited 5-35% incidence) and desmopressin management - on-topic and valid.

stem: A 31-year-old woman (G3P2, BMI 34) at 39 weeks is undergoing induction of labor for postdates. Shortly after artificial rupture of membranes she becomes acutely dyspneic and agitated, then suddenly unresponsive. Monitors show SpO2 70%, no palpable pulse, and the rhythm is pulseless electrical activity. Fetal heart tracing shows a prolonged deceleration to 60/min. A code is called and high-quality CPR is begun with the uterus manually displaced to the left. The fetus has not yet delivered. In addition to continuing chest compressions and securing the airway, which intervention is most likely to improve both maternal resuscitation and fetal outcome if return of spontaneous circulation is not achieved promptly?

choices.text: Perform resuscitative hysterotomy (perimortem cesarean delivery) with a goal of delivery by about 5 minutes of arrest
choices.rationale: Correct: in maternal cardiac arrest at a clearly viable gestation, evacuating the gravid uterus relieves aortocaval compression and improves the efficacy of compressions, so delivery should be targeted near 4-5 minutes of arrest rather than delayed.

choices.text: Increase the left lateral tilt of the entire body to 30 degrees to optimize chest compression while relieving caval compression
choices.rationale: Incorrect: tilting the torso degrades the quality and force of chest compressions, which is why manual left uterine displacement (not body tilt) is recommended during CPR in pregnancy.

choices.text: Withhold defibrillation and standard ACLS drug doses until after the fetus is delivered to avoid fetal exposure
choices.rationale: Incorrect: standard ACLS energy levels and drug doses are used unchanged in the pregnant patient, and resuscitation should never be withheld out of concern for the fetus.

choices.text: Administer high-dose corticosteroids as the priority pharmacologic therapy for the suspected embolic event
choices.rationale: Incorrect: corticosteroids are not an evidence-based, time-critical intervention in amniotic fluid embolism and do not address the immediate cause of arrest, which is circulatory collapse requiring CPR and delivery.

choices.text: Delay any operative delivery until a definitive diagnosis is confirmed by serum tryptase and a fibrinogen level
choices.rationale: Incorrect: amniotic fluid embolism is a clinical diagnosis of exclusion, and waiting for laboratory confirmation would fatally delay the resuscitative hysterotomy that the situation demands.

explanation: Sudden cardiovascular collapse, hypoxia, and DIC around the time of membrane rupture or delivery is the classic presentation of amniotic fluid embolism, the leading cause of peripartum cardiac arrest. Management is supportive and time-critical: high-quality CPR with manual left uterine displacement, unchanged ACLS doses and defibrillation energies, and resuscitative hysterotomy targeted by roughly 4-5 minutes of arrest if there is no ROSC. Emptying the uterus relieves aortocaval compression and dramatically improves maternal compression hemodynamics, which is why it benefits the mother even independent of fetal considerations.
subtopic: Amniotic fluid embolism and maternal cardiac arrest (resuscitative hysterotomy)

verdict.reason: CLINICAL: Stem is a textbook AFE presentation (sudden collapse, hypoxia, PEA arrest at ARM during induction at viable gestation). The keyed answer (resuscitative hysterotomy/perimortem cesarean, delivery targeted by ~4-5 min of arrest with no ROSC) is the single best answer per AHA and SOAP/SMFM guidance: uterine evacuation relieves aortocaval compression, improves compression hemodynamics, and benefits both mother and fetus. All distractors are genuinely inferior and correctly explained: (1) 30-degree whole-body tilt degrades compression quality vs. manual LUD; (2) ACLS energies/drug doses are unchanged in pregnancy and resuscitation is never withheld for the fetus; (3) corticosteroids are not an evidence-based, time-critical AFE therapy; (4) AFE is a clinical diagnosis of exclusion and awaiting tryptase/fibrinogen would fatally delay PMCD. The answer is unambiguous. The "4-5 minute" framing is standard (initiate by ~4 min, deliver by ~5 min); the StatPearls "1-2 minute" wording refers to procedural execution speed and does not contradict the key. REFERENCE: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK559107/ resolves to the StatPearls 'Amniotic Fluid Embolism' chapter, which explicitly covers AFE management, maternal cardiac arrest, resuscitative hysterotomy for viable gestation, manual left uterine displacement, and ACLS in pregnancy. Citation is accurate and directly on-topic.

stem: A 28-year-old healthy parturient (G1P0) at 38 weeks is positioned supine on the operating table in preparation for placement of a spinal anesthetic for elective cesarean delivery. Before any block is performed, she reports nausea and lightheadedness. Her noninvasive blood pressure, which was 122/74 mmHg on arrival, now reads 86/48 mmHg, and the fetal heart rate tracing shows a new deceleration. She has received no medications. Which intervention most directly addresses the underlying cause of her hypotension?

choices.text: Administer a 500 mL crystalloid co-load and recheck the pressure in 10 minutes
choices.rationale: Incorrect: while fluids may help support venous return, they do not relieve the mechanical compression that is the primary cause, and waiting 10 minutes risks ongoing fetal compromise.

choices.text: Apply manual left uterine displacement or place a wedge under the right hip to tilt her left
choices.rationale: Correct: in the supine term parturient the gravid uterus compresses the inferior vena cava and aorta, and displacing the uterus off the great vessels restores venous return and cardiac output, directly treating supine hypotensive syndrome.

choices.text: Give a 0.5 mg IV bolus of atropine to treat the presumed vagally mediated bradycardia
choices.rationale: Incorrect: the maternal problem is reduced preload from caval compression, not a primary bradyarrhythmia, so atropine does not address the cause and the fetal deceleration reflects reduced uteroplacental flow.

choices.text: Place the patient in steep Trendelenburg to autotransfuse blood from the lower extremities
choices.rationale: Incorrect: Trendelenburg does not relieve uterine compression of the vena cava and can worsen respiratory mechanics and cephalad spread once a block is placed, without correcting the mechanical obstruction.

choices.text: Proceed immediately with the spinal anesthetic so surgery can relieve the compression by delivering the fetus
choices.rationale: Incorrect: placing a sympathectomy-producing spinal on top of existing aortocaval compression would compound the hypotension, and the obstruction must be relieved by repositioning first.

explanation: After about 20 weeks the gravid uterus can compress the IVC and aorta when the patient is supine, reducing venous return and cardiac output and producing supine hypotensive syndrome with maternal symptoms and fetal heart rate changes. The defining treatment is mechanical: manual left uterine displacement or a 15-degree left tilt with a wedge under the right hip. Fluids and vasopressors are adjuncts that support pressure but do not remove the obstruction, and the deceleration reflects compromised uteroplacental perfusion that resolves once the vessels are decompressed.
subtopic: Aortocaval compression / supine hypotensive syndrome and positioning

verdict.reason: Clinical content is sound and unambiguous. The stem is a textbook supine hypotensive syndrome / aortocaval compression scenario in a term parturient (hypotension, nausea/lightheadedness, fetal deceleration, supine, no block, no meds). The keyed answer B (manual left uterine displacement / right hip wedge) is the single best answer because the stem asks for the intervention that "most directly addresses the underlying cause" — i.e., the mechanical relief of IVC/aortic compression. Distractors are all genuinely inferior: A (fluid + 10-min delay) is an adjunct that doesn't relieve compression and dangerously delays; C (atropine) treats a vagal bradyarrhythmia that isn't the mechanism; D (steep Trendelenburg) doesn't decompress the IVC and worsens respiratory mechanics/block spread; E (proceed with spinal) adds sympathectomy on top of existing compression. Rationales and explanation are accurate and current (compression after ~20 weeks, 15-degree left tilt/LUD as defining treatment, deceleration from impaired uteroplacental perfusion). REFERENCE: WebFetch of https://www.openanesthesia.org/keywords/uteroplacental-circulation/ resolves (not 404), title 'Uteroplacental Circulation - OpenAnesthesia', and explicitly covers aortocaval compression in the supine position and mitigation via 15-degree tilt / manual uterine displacement — directly on-topic. The only nuance is that in clinical practice LUD is done together with fluids/vasopressors, but the stem's 'most directly addresses the underlying cause' phrasing cleanly disambiguates to B.

stem: A 33-year-old parturient at 39 weeks with no significant comorbidities receives a spinal anesthetic for elective cesarean delivery. A sensory level to T4 is confirmed. Within 3 minutes her blood pressure falls from a baseline of 118/72 mmHg to 84/50 mmHg; she is asymptomatic, the heart rate is 92/min, and the fetal status is currently reassuring. You plan a vasopressor to maintain maternal pressure near baseline and optimize uteroplacental perfusion and fetal acid-base status. Which agent and rationale is most appropriate?

choices.text: Ephedrine infusion, because its beta effects increase cardiac output and better preserve umbilical artery pH than alpha agonists
choices.rationale: Incorrect: ephedrine crosses the placenta and stimulates fetal metabolism, and trials show it is associated with lower (more acidotic) umbilical artery pH than phenylephrine.

choices.text: Phenylephrine, because it restores maternal perfusion pressure while producing less fetal acidosis than ephedrine when maternal cardiac output is maintained
choices.rationale: Correct: phenylephrine is the preferred first-line agent for spinal-induced hypotension at cesarean delivery because it maintains maternal blood pressure with better-preserved fetal pH, provided reflex bradycardia and falling cardiac output are watched for and treated.

choices.text: Low-dose epinephrine infusion, because its mixed activity is the standard of care for routine spinal hypotension in obstetrics
choices.rationale: Incorrect: epinephrine is not a routine first-line vasopressor for neuraxial hypotension at cesarean delivery and adds tachyarrhythmia and metabolic effects without an established fetal benefit over phenylephrine.

choices.text: Dopamine infusion titrated to a renal-dose range to selectively improve uteroplacental blood flow
choices.rationale: Incorrect: there is no selective uteroplacental vasodilation from dopamine, the renal-dose concept is discredited, and it is not used to treat spinal hypotension in this setting.

choices.text: Withhold all vasopressors and rely on a rapid crystalloid bolus alone, since vasopressors reduce uterine blood flow
choices.rationale: Incorrect: leaving sustained maternal hypotension untreated reduces uteroplacental perfusion pressure and risks fetal acidosis, and maintaining maternal pressure with phenylephrine preserves rather than harms placental flow.

explanation: Spinal anesthesia for cesarean delivery commonly causes sympathectomy-mediated hypotension that lowers uteroplacental perfusion pressure. Phenylephrine is the preferred first-line vasopressor: by maintaining maternal blood pressure without the fetal metabolic stimulation seen with ephedrine, it is associated with better-preserved umbilical artery pH. The main caveat is that phenylephrine's reflex bradycardia can lower maternal cardiac output, so the goal is to keep pressure near baseline while monitoring heart rate and output.
subtopic: Uteroplacental perfusion and vasopressor selection (phenylephrine vs ephedrine, fetal acid-base)

verdict.reason: CLINICAL: Sound and current. Keyed answer (phenylephrine, index 1) is the consensus first-line vasopressor for spinal-induced hypotension at cesarean delivery (2018 international consensus statement; Ngan Kee meta-analyses), associated with higher/better-preserved umbilical artery pH than ephedrine, which crosses the placenta and causes fetal metabolic stimulation/acidosis. The rationale correctly flags the reflex-bradycardia/reduced-cardiac-output caveat. All distractors are genuinely inferior: ephedrine's reasoning (index 0) reflects the outdated belief and is correctly refuted; low-dose epinephrine (index 2) is not routine first-line; renal-dose dopamine (index 3) is discredited with no selective uteroplacental vasodilation; withholding vasopressors (index 4) leaves harmful hypotension untreated. The stem is unambiguous — BP 84/50 confirms clinically significant hypotension, HR 92 is normal/slightly elevated (not bradycardic, supporting phenylephrine tolerability), fetal status reassuring. Explanation is accurate. REFERENCE: WebFetch of https://www.openanesthesia.org/keywords/uteroplacental-circulation/ resolved (live page, updated 02/11/2026) and is directly on-topic — it explicitly states the uteroplacental bed lacks autoregulation and is BP-dependent, recommends phenylephrine infusion 50-100 mcg/min to maintain MAP, and states phenylephrine preserves fetal pH better than ephedrine. Both checks pass.

stem: A 34-year-old nonsmoking woman with a history of motion sickness presents for elective outpatient laparoscopic cholecystectomy. She has no prior surgical history. The anesthetic plan is a sevoflurane-based general anesthetic, and the surgeon anticipates she will need oral opioids for postoperative pain at home. Using the simplified Apfel score to risk-stratify her, which combination of interventions is the MOST appropriate prophylactic strategy for this patient?

choices.text: No prophylaxis; treat with rescue ondansetron only if she develops nausea in the PACU
choices.rationale: Incorrect, because with three to four risk factors her predicted PONV risk is roughly 60-80%, so withholding prophylaxis is inappropriate for a high-risk patient.

choices.text: A single dose of ondansetron 4 mg IV at the end of surgery
choices.rationale: Incorrect, because monotherapy is inadequate for a high-risk patient; guidelines call for combining agents of differing mechanisms when two or more risk factors are present.

choices.text: Two antiemetics of different classes (e.g., dexamethasone at induction plus ondansetron at end of case) combined with a propofol-based total intravenous technique
choices.rationale: Correct, because a high-risk patient warrants multimodal prophylaxis with two-plus agents of differing mechanisms plus a baseline-risk-lowering strategy such as TIVA and opioid minimization.

choices.text: Scopolamine patch alone applied in the PACU after emergence
choices.rationale: Incorrect, because transdermal scopolamine has a 2-4 hour onset and is single-agent therapy, making it inadequate and poorly timed for a high-risk patient.

choices.text: Metoclopramide 10 mg IV as the sole prophylactic agent
choices.rationale: Incorrect, because the standard 10 mg dose of metoclopramide has not been shown to be an effective PONV prophylactic, let alone as monotherapy in a high-risk patient.

explanation: This patient has four Apfel risk factors (female sex, nonsmoker, history of motion sickness, and anticipated postoperative opioids), predicting an approximately 80% baseline PONV risk. Current consensus guidelines recommend that high-risk patients receive multimodal prophylaxis: at least two antiemetics from different pharmacologic classes combined with techniques that lower baseline risk, such as propofol-based TIVA, avoidance of volatile agents and nitrous oxide, and opioid-sparing analgesia. Dexamethasone is best given at induction (it has a slow onset), whereas 5-HT3 antagonists like ondansetron are most effective when given near the end of surgery.
subtopic: Postoperative nausea and vomiting risk stratification and multimodal prophylaxis
reference_title: Postoperative Nausea and Vomiting in Adults – OpenAnesthesia

verdict.reason: Clinical content is correct and unambiguous. The patient has 4 Apfel simplified risk factors (female, nonsmoker, history of motion sickness, anticipated postoperative opioids) = ~80% PONV risk, a high-risk patient. The keyed answer (index 2: two antiemetics of different classes — dexamethasone at induction + ondansetron at end of case — plus propofol-based TIVA) is the single best answer and aligns with the Fourth Consensus PONV Guidelines / OpenAnesthesia, which call for >=2 antiemetics from different classes combined with baseline-risk-reduction (TIVA, opioid/volatile/N2O minimization) in high-risk patients. Dexamethasone-at-induction and ondansetron-at-end-of-case timing is accurate and current. All distractors are genuinely inferior: no prophylaxis (inappropriate for high-risk), single ondansetron (monotherapy inadequate), scopolamine alone in PACU (single agent, poorly timed given 2-4h onset), and metoclopramide 10 mg sole agent (standard dose ineffective for PONV prophylaxis). No two defensible answers. REFERENCE: WebFetch of the OpenAnesthesia URL resolves (last updated Oct 2023, titled 'Postoperative Nausea and Vomiting in Adults') and is directly on-topic — it explicitly states the 0-4 factor risks (~10/20/40/60/80%) and recommends >=2 antiemetics plus TIVA/opioid minimization for multiple risk factors. Both clinical and reference are sound.

stem: A 28-year-old healthy woman is recovering after outpatient knee arthroscopy under general anesthesia. Ninety minutes after arrival in phase II recovery, she is fully awake and oriented, ambulates to the bathroom with steady gait, has stable vital signs within 20% of baseline, reports a pain score of 2/10 controlled with oral analgesics, and has had no nausea or vomiting. She has not yet voided and has minimal surgical-site drainage. Which statement BEST reflects appropriate use of a discharge-readiness scoring system such as the Post-Anesthetic Discharge Scoring System (PADSS) for this patient?

choices.text: She must demonstrate spontaneous urination before discharge can be considered, regardless of her procedure or risk category
choices.rationale: Incorrect, because mandatory voiding is no longer universally required and is reserved for higher-risk patients; low-risk patients may be discharged with instructions to seek care if unable to void.

choices.text: She must be able to tolerate oral fluids before meeting discharge criteria, as mandatory drinking is a core PADSS requirement
choices.rationale: Incorrect, because routine mandatory oral intake is not required for discharge and may actually increase PONV; it is not a gating criterion in modern PADSS practice.

choices.text: She can be assessed for home discharge using PADSS criteria, and meeting an adequate score across vital signs, ambulation, pain, nausea, and bleeding supports readiness even without documented voiding
choices.rationale: Correct, because PADSS scores stable vital signs, activity/ambulation, nausea/vomiting, pain, and surgical bleeding, and a passing score supports home discharge in a low-risk patient without mandatory voiding or drinking.

choices.text: She must remain a minimum of four hours in recovery before any discharge scoring tool can be applied
choices.rationale: Incorrect, because there is no fixed mandatory observation interval; discharge depends on meeting validated criteria, not on a predetermined clock minimum.

choices.text: She requires a documented modified Aldrete score before PADSS can be used, because PADSS only applies to phase I PACU patients
choices.rationale: Incorrect, because PADSS is specifically designed for home-readiness (phase II) assessment, whereas the modified Aldrete score is typically applied earlier for phase I recovery.

explanation: The Post-Anesthetic Discharge Scoring System (PADSS) assesses readiness for home discharge across five domains: vital signs, ambulation, nausea/vomiting, pain, and surgical bleeding, each scored 0-2, with a total of 9 or greater generally indicating readiness. Contemporary practice has moved away from mandatory voiding and mandatory oral intake as universal prerequisites; these are reserved for selected higher-risk patients (e.g., spinal/epidural anesthesia, urologic or pelvic surgery, history of urinary retention). A low-risk patient like this who meets the scored criteria can be discharged with appropriate instructions even if she has not yet voided.
subtopic: Postanesthesia recovery and discharge readiness (PADSS / fast-tracking)

verdict.reason: CLINICAL: Keyed answer (index 2) is the single best, unambiguous answer. PADSS is a phase II home-readiness tool scoring vital signs, activity/ambulation, nausea/vomiting, pain, and surgical bleeding (0-2 each), and modern revised PADSS removed mandatory voiding and oral intake as universal prerequisites (reserved for higher-risk patients). All four distractors are genuinely inferior and reflect real misconceptions: mandatory voiding for all (A), mandatory oral intake (B), a fixed 4-hour observation minimum (D), and the false claim that PADSS is phase I only / requires a prior Aldrete score (E). The explanation's '9 or greater' threshold matches the standard Marshall & Chung 10-point PADSS cutoff and is correct, though not stated verbatim on the cited page. REFERENCE: WebFetch of https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK594237/ resolves to the StatPearls 'Aldrete Scoring System' article (title matches reference_title). It is directly on-topic, covering PADSS, modified Aldrete, fast-tracking, and the phase I vs II distinction, and explicitly states that revisions of PADSS 'removed the oral fluid intake and voiding before discharge criteria' — directly supporting the load-bearing clinical claim. Both clinical content and citation are sound.

stem: A 74-year-old man (ASA III, controlled HTN and a drug-eluting coronary stent placed 2 years ago) undergoes transurethral resection of the prostate under spinal anesthesia with a glycine 1.5% irrigation solution. About 55 minutes into the resection he becomes restless and confused, complains of nausea and transient blurred vision, and develops a blood pressure of 168/94 mmHg followed by bradycardia. Stat labs: serum sodium 116 mEq/L, serum osmolality 248 mOsm/kg. He is breathing spontaneously and SpO2 is 97% on supplemental oxygen. Which is the MOST appropriate next step?

choices.text: Ask the surgeon to terminate the resection and administer hypertonic 3% saline as a 100 mL (1.5-2 mL/kg) bolus over ~10 minutes, with furosemide, targeting a sodium rise of no more than ~10 mEq/L in 24 hours
choices.rationale: Correct: symptomatic dilutional hyponatremia from glycine absorption is treated by stopping fluid absorption and giving a controlled hypertonic saline bolus while capping correction near 8-10 mEq/L per 24 h to avoid osmotic demyelination.

choices.text: Continue the resection but switch the irrigant to sterile distilled water to reduce further sodium loss
choices.rationale: Incorrect: distilled water is hypo-osmolar and causes hemolysis and even more profound hyponatremia, and the resection should be stopped, not continued.

choices.text: Rapidly infuse 1 L of 0.9% normal saline wide open to restore the sodium quickly
choices.rationale: Incorrect: isotonic saline corrects severe symptomatic hyponatremia too slowly and adds free water/volume, whereas this neurologically symptomatic patient needs hypertonic saline.

choices.text: Give a 1 mg/kg bolus of intravenous lidocaine to treat presumed local anesthetic systemic toxicity
choices.rationale: Incorrect: the hyponatremia, hypo-osmolality, and visual symptoms point to TURP syndrome rather than LAST, and lidocaine would not address the dilutional state.

choices.text: Restrict all fluids and simply observe, since neurologic symptoms from hyponatremia resolve spontaneously once irrigation stops
choices.rationale: Incorrect: with a sodium of 116 mEq/L and active CNS symptoms, fluid restriction alone is inadequate and delays treatment of a potentially life-threatening cerebral edema state.

explanation: TURP (and hysteroscopic) syndrome results from systemic absorption of hypotonic, electrolyte-free irrigant through open prostatic venous sinuses, producing dilutional hyponatremia with hypo-osmolality, cerebral edema, and (with glycine) hyperammonemia and transient visual disturbance. Management of the symptomatic patient is to stop the procedure and absorption, give hypertonic 3% saline as a small bolus (about 100 mL or 1.5-2 mL/kg over ~10 minutes) with a loop diuretic, and limit correction to roughly 8-10 mEq/L per 24 hours to prevent osmotic demyelination. Using risk-reduction measures such as limiting resection time, irrigant pressure/height, and switching to isotonic irrigants with bipolar resection lowers the incidence.
subtopic: Urology: TURP/hysteroscopy syndrome and irrigant-induced dilutional hyponatremia
reference_title: Hyponatremia and Transurethral Resection of Prostate Syndrome - OpenAnesthesia

verdict.reason: CLINICAL: Sound and unambiguous. The patient has severe symptomatic dilutional hyponatremia (Na 116, osmolality 248 confirming true hypotonic state) with clear CNS symptoms (restlessness, confusion, nausea, visual disturbance) and a Cushing-type HTN-then-bradycardia pattern from glycine irrigant absorption during TURP — classic TURP syndrome. The keyed answer (stop resection + 3% hypertonic saline ~100 mL/1.5-2 mL/kg bolus over ~10 min with furosemide, capping correction at ~8-10 mEq/L/24h to prevent osmotic demyelination) is the single best answer and reflects current practice; the 100 mL bolus is consistent with standard 100-150 mL 3% saline boluses for severe symptomatic hyponatremia. All four distractors are genuinely inferior: distilled water causes hemolysis/worse hyponatremia and continuing is wrong; 0.9% saline wide open corrects too slowly and adds free water/volume for a neurologically symptomatic patient; lidocaine treats LAST, not the dilutional state; and fluid restriction/observation alone is inadequate at Na 116 with active CNS symptoms. Rationales and explanation are accurate. REFERENCE: WebFetch confirms the URL resolves to the OpenAnesthesia page 'Hyponatermia and Transurethral Resection of Prostate Syndrome,' directly on-topic for TURP syndrome and irrigant-induced hyponatremia. The URL contains a misspelling ('hyponatermia') but it still resolves correctly to the matching page, so it is not a broken/mismatched citation. Both clinical and reference checks pass.

stem: An 82-year-old woman with osteoporosis, a remote MI 3 years ago (asymptomatic, on a beta-blocker), and well-controlled hypertension is undergoing a cemented bipolar hemiarthroplasty for a displaced femoral neck fracture under general anesthesia. Immediately after the surgeon pressurizes methylmethacrylate cement and impacts the femoral prosthesis into the canal, her SpO2 falls from 99% to 86%, end-tidal CO2 drops from 35 to 22 mmHg, and blood pressure falls from 128/72 to 74/40 mmHg with new sinus tachycardia. Which underlying mechanism is the MOST likely explanation for this deterioration?

choices.text: Embolization of marrow fat, cement particles, and air into the pulmonary circulation, causing an acute rise in pulmonary vascular resistance and right ventricular strain
choices.rationale: Correct: bone cement implantation syndrome arises when femoral canal pressurization forces marrow/fat, cement debris, and air into the venous circulation, producing pulmonary embolic load, increased PVR, and right heart dysfunction with hypotension, hypoxemia, and a falling EtCO2.

choices.text: An acute IgE-mediated anaphylactic reaction to the methylmethacrylate monomer
choices.rationale: Incorrect: true IgE-mediated anaphylaxis to MMA is exceedingly rare, and the embolic/RV-strain picture (falling EtCO2 with hypoxemia at the moment of prosthesis impaction) fits BCIS rather than anaphylaxis.

choices.text: Acute left ventricular failure from the exothermic heat of cement polymerization transmitted to the myocardium
choices.rationale: Incorrect: polymerization heat is local to the femoral canal and does not reach or directly injure the heart, and the hemodynamics reflect a right-sided embolic process.

choices.text: Profound vasodilation and bradycardia from systemic absorption of residual MMA monomer acting as a direct myocardial depressant
choices.rationale: Incorrect: while absorbed monomer can contribute mildly to vasodilation, the dominant and classic mechanism of acute BCIS collapse is embolic pulmonary hypertension, and this patient is tachycardic rather than bradycardic.

choices.text: A large-volume venous air embolism entrained through the open surgical wound from a sub-atmospheric venous pressure gradient
choices.rationale: Incorrect: although air is one embolic component, an isolated wound-entrained air embolism is not the principal mechanism here, since cement pressurization actively drives a mixed marrow-fat-cement-air embolic load into the canal veins.

explanation: Bone cement implantation syndrome (BCIS) classically occurs at cementation, prosthesis insertion, or reduction during cemented arthroplasty, especially cemented hip hemiarthroplasty for fracture in elderly, comorbid patients. Pressurization of the femoral canal embolizes marrow fat, cement, and air into the pulmonary vasculature, abruptly raising pulmonary vascular resistance and causing hypoxemia, a sharp fall in EtCO2 (from increased dead space and reduced cardiac output), hypotension, and right ventricular strain that can progress to arrest. Anticipatory management includes raising FiO2 to 100%, ensuring euvolemia before cementing, communicating with the surgeon, using vasopressors for the right heart, and considering uncemented techniques in the highest-risk patients.

stem: A healthy 29-year-old undergoes arthroscopic ACL reconstruction under general anesthesia with a thigh pneumatic tourniquet that has been inflated to 300 mmHg for 105 minutes. Ventilation has been steady at a fixed minute ventilation. Immediately after the surgeon deflates the tourniquet, you should anticipate which set of transient physiologic changes?

choices.text: A rise in end-tidal CO2 peaking at 1-3 minutes, a transient fall in mean arterial pressure and core temperature, and a rise in serum potassium and lactate
choices.rationale: Correct: deflation washes hypercapnic, hyperkalemic, acidotic, lactate-laden venous blood from the ischemic limb into the central circulation, producing a brief EtCO2 spike, hypotension from volume redistribution and metabolite washout, a drop in core temperature, and transient hyperkalemia.

choices.text: A fall in end-tidal CO2, a rise in mean arterial pressure, and a fall in serum potassium
choices.rationale: Incorrect: this is the opposite of reperfusion physiology, since washout of CO2-rich, potassium-rich ischemic blood raises EtCO2 and potassium and lowers, not raises, arterial pressure.

choices.text: A rise in core temperature, a rise in mean arterial pressure, and unchanged end-tidal CO2
choices.rationale: Incorrect: cool ischemic blood returning to the core lowers temperature, deflation lowers blood pressure, and EtCO2 rises rather than staying unchanged.

choices.text: An abrupt rise in mean arterial pressure and heart rate identical to the tourniquet pain that appears during prolonged inflation
choices.rationale: Incorrect: the hypertension and tachycardia of tourniquet pain occur during inflation (typically after 30-60 minutes), whereas deflation characteristically causes a fall in arterial pressure.

choices.text: No measurable systemic change, because the metabolic effects of limb ischemia remain confined to the extremity after deflation
choices.rationale: Incorrect: deflation specifically releases the sequestered metabolites systemically, and the EtCO2, potassium, lactate, blood pressure, and temperature changes are measurable and well described.

explanation: Inflation of a pneumatic tourniquet sequesters the limb's metabolic byproducts; deflation washes hypercapnic, acidotic, hyperkalemic, lactate-rich venous blood into the systemic circulation. The result is a transient EtCO2 rise that peaks at about 1-3 minutes and returns to baseline within roughly 10-13 minutes in spontaneously breathing patients, a fall in central venous pressure and mean arterial pressure (occasionally severe), a drop in core temperature from returning cool blood, and transient rises in serum potassium and lactate. Anticipating these changes lets the anesthetist transiently increase minute ventilation, ensure euvolemia, and be prepared to support blood pressure at the moment of release; tourniquet pain with hypertension and tachycardia, by contrast, is a feature of prolonged inflation (about 30-60 minutes).
subtopic: Orthopedics: pneumatic tourniquet physiology and systemic effects of deflation
reference_title: Tourniquet application during anesthesia: What we need to know? (PMC)

Citation is valid and on-topic.

stem: A 64-year-old man with COPD (FEV1 52% predicted, baseline SpO2 93% on room air) and a BMI of 36 kg/m2 is scheduled for arthroscopic rotator cuff repair. The team plans an ultrasound-guided single-injection interscalene brachial plexus block with 20 mL of 0.5% ropivacaine for postoperative analgesia. Forty minutes after an uneventful block, in the PACU, the patient reports new mild dyspnea; SpO2 has drifted to 88% on 2 L/min nasal cannula, and he is using accessory muscles when supine. Breath sounds are diminished at the right base. He is hemodynamically stable and the surgical arm is well-analgesed. Which mechanism most likely explains his respiratory deterioration?

choices.text: Ipsilateral phrenic nerve blockade causing hemidiaphragmatic paresis
choices.rationale: Correct: the phrenic nerve runs on the anterior scalene immediately adjacent to the plexus, so interscalene block almost always spreads to it, and hemidiaphragmatic paresis is poorly tolerated when pulmonary reserve is already reduced by COPD and obesity.

choices.text: Local-anesthetic-induced bronchospasm from ropivacaine systemic absorption
choices.rationale: Incorrect: ropivacaine does not cause bronchospasm at these doses, and the picture is diaphragmatic splinting (worse supine, basal diminution) rather than diffuse wheezing.

choices.text: Pneumothorax from needle entry into the pleura
choices.rationale: Incorrect: while possible, an interscalene approach is well above the dome of the pleura, and the gradual onset of dyspnea over 40 minutes with stable hemodynamics fits diaphragmatic paresis far better than an expanding pneumothorax.

choices.text: Local anesthetic systemic toxicity (LAST) causing central respiratory depression
choices.rationale: Incorrect: LAST presents with neurologic prodrome (tinnitus, perioral numbness, seizures) or cardiovascular collapse and typically appears within minutes of injection, not as isolated positional dyspnea 40 minutes later.

choices.text: Recurrent laryngeal nerve block causing upper-airway obstruction
choices.rationale: Incorrect: recurrent laryngeal nerve block produces hoarseness and a sense of throat fullness, not basal hypoventilation with hypoxemia worsened by the supine position.

explanation: Ipsilateral phrenic nerve palsy occurs in nearly 100% of conventional-volume interscalene blocks because the phrenic nerve lies on the anterior scalene muscle just anterior to the C5-C6 roots. Healthy patients tolerate the resulting 25-30% drop in pulmonary function, but those with COPD, obesity, or other limited reserve can become symptomatically hypoxemic, especially supine. Management is supportive (upright positioning, oxygen) since the deficit resolves as the block regresses; risk is reduced by low-volume, low-dose, or more distal (e.g., supraclavicular or superior trunk) techniques.

All distractors are genuinely inferior: (B) ropivacaine does not cause bronchospasm at these doses and the picture is splinting not wheeze; (C) pneumothorax is rare from the interscalene approach (needle well above the pleural dome — more a supraclavicular concern) and an expanding pneumothorax is not classically positional and would tend to destabilize hemodynamics; (D) LAST presents with a neuro prodrome/CV collapse within minutes, not isolated positional dyspnea at 40 min; (E) recurrent laryngeal nerve block causes hoarseness, not basal hypoventilation. stem: A 29-year-old woman receives a spinal anesthetic with hyperbaric bupivacaine for a scheduled cesarean delivery. Shortly after injection she reports that her feet feel warm and she can no longer sense a cold alcohol swab on her lower abdomen, yet she can still move her toes and feels firm pressure when the skin is pinched. The obstetrician asks you to confirm the block is progressing as expected before incision. Which statement best explains the sequence of sensory and motor changes she is experiencing?

choices.text: Sympathetic (vasomotor) fibers are blocked first, producing warmth and an early loss of temperature sensation, while motor fibers are among the last to be blocked
choices.rationale: Correct: small B and C fibers carrying sympathetic and temperature signals are most susceptible to local anesthetic, so vasodilatory warmth and loss of cold sensation appear before the larger A-alpha motor fibers are blocked.

choices.text: Large motor (A-alpha) fibers are blocked first because their greater surface area absorbs more local anesthetic
choices.rationale: Incorrect: larger myelinated fibers are relatively resistant and are blocked late, which is why motor function is preserved early in the differential block.

choices.text: All fiber types are blocked simultaneously, so the warmth simply reflects the patient's anxiety rather than a neural sequence
choices.rationale: Incorrect: local anesthetics produce a reproducible differential block by fiber type, not simultaneous onset, and the warmth reflects genuine sympathetic blockade.

choices.text: The preserved pressure sensation indicates a failed block that should be repeated immediately
choices.rationale: Incorrect: deep pressure and proprioception (large A fibers) are blocked late, so their persistence early is expected and does not signify failure.

choices.text: Pain fibers are the most resistant, so analgesia will be the last modality to develop
choices.rationale: Incorrect: small unmyelinated pain fibers are among the earliest blocked, so analgesia develops early rather than last.

explanation: Local anesthetics block nerve fibers differentially according to size, myelination, and firing frequency. Small autonomic (B) and temperature/pain (C and A-delta) fibers are blocked at lower concentrations and earlier than large A-alpha motor and proprioceptive fibers, giving the classic clinical progression: sympathetic (warmth, vasodilation) > temperature > pain > touch/proprioception > motor. The sensory level for surgical anesthesia is typically two or more segments higher than the motor level, and the autonomic block extends higher still.
subtopic: Local anesthetic pharmacology - differential neural blockade and fiber sensitivity

Keyed answer (index 0) correctly states the differential block sequence: small sympathetic B fibers and temperature/pain (C, A-delta) fibers are blocked first at lower LA concentrations, producing early warmth/vasodilation and loss of cold sensation, while large A-alpha motor fibers are blocked last. The stem's snapshot (warm feet, loss of cold sensation, preserved toe movement, preserved deep pressure on pinch) is internally consistent with an early progressing block. All four distractors are genuinely inferior and correctly explained: (1) large A-alpha fibers are resistant/blocked late, not first; (2) differential block by fiber type is reproducible, not simultaneous, and the warmth is real sympathetic blockade not anxiety; (3) preserved pressure/proprioception (large fibers blocked late) is expected, not block failure; (4) small pain fibers are blocked early, so analgesia develops early not last. The explanation's clinical ordering and the statement that sensory level sits ~2+ segments above motor with autonomic higher still are accurate and current.
