No clinical, reference, or ambiguity problems found.

stem: A 9-month-old, 8-kg infant with unrepaired tetralogy of Fallot is brought to the operating room for an inguinal hernia repair. Shortly after an inhalational induction with sevoflurane, the child becomes acutely agitated and then deeply cyanotic. SpO2 falls from 88% to 62%, and the previously loud systolic ejection murmur heard at the left upper sternal border becomes markedly softer. Heart rate is 178/min and the blood pressure is 64/38 mmHg. Which intervention most directly targets the primary mechanism of this episode?

choices.text: Administer IV phenylephrine 5–10 mcg/kg and manually flex the hips and knees toward the chest
choices.rationale: Correct: a hypercyanotic 'Tet' spell is driven by a fall in systemic vascular resistance (and/or dynamic RVOT obstruction) that increases right-to-left shunting, so raising SVR with phenylephrine plus the knee-to-chest position restores forward pulmonary blood flow.

choices.text: Give IV nitroglycerin to reduce right ventricular afterload and improve pulmonary flow
choices.rationale: Incorrect: nitroglycerin lowers systemic vascular resistance, which worsens the right-to-left shunt and deepens cyanosis in a Tet spell.

choices.text: Deepen anesthesia with an IV bolus of propofol to relieve the dynamic infundibular spasm
choices.rationale: Incorrect: propofol causes systemic vasodilation and hypotension, dropping SVR and aggravating the right-to-left shunt despite any effect on infundibular tone.

choices.text: Administer IV atropine to treat the tachycardia and improve diastolic filling
choices.rationale: Incorrect: atropine increases heart rate and would worsen the dynamic infundibular obstruction; the tachycardia here is a response to hypoxia, not the target.

choices.text: Start an isoproterenol infusion to augment cardiac output and pulmonary blood flow
choices.rationale: Incorrect: isoproterenol is a beta-agonist that increases infundibular contractility and lowers SVR, both of which intensify the right-to-left shunt and are contraindicated in a Tet spell.

explanation: Hypercyanotic ('Tet') spells in tetralogy of Fallot result from increased right-to-left shunting across the VSD, precipitated by a fall in systemic vascular resistance and/or dynamic right ventricular outflow tract (infundibular) obstruction; the softening murmur reflects reduced flow across the RVOT. Management raises SVR and reduces shunt: knee-to-chest positioning, 100% oxygen, IV fluids, phenylephrine, and esmolol/beta-blockade to relax the infundibulum, while opioids/sedation reduce the catecholamine surge. Agents that lower SVR (nitroglycerin, propofol) or increase infundibular contractility and heart rate (isoproterenol, atropine) worsen the episode.
subtopic: Congenital heart disease: hypercyanotic (Tet) spell management in tetralogy of Fallot

verdict.reason: CLINICAL: Sound and unambiguous. The stem is a textbook hypercyanotic ("Tet") spell during sevoflurane induction in unrepaired TOF — agitation, deep cyanosis (SpO2 88→62%), and crucially a SOFTENING ejection murmur, which correctly reflects reduced flow across the RVOT (the murmur originates from pulmonary stenosis, not the VSD) as right-to-left shunting increases. The keyed answer (phenylephrine 5–10 mcg/kg IV + knee-to-chest) is the single best of the five options: it directly raises SVR to reduce R→L shunt, and the dose is appropriate pediatric Tet-spell dosing. All four distractors are genuinely inferior and their rationales are accurate: nitroglycerin and propofol lower SVR and worsen the shunt; atropine raises HR/worsens dynamic infundibular obstruction (tachycardia here is compensatory); isoproterenol increases infundibular contractility and lowers SVR (contraindicated). The only other defensible mechanism-targeted agent (esmolol/beta-blockade for infundibular spasm) is NOT among the choices, so there is no competing correct option — no true ambiguity. The explanation is accurate and current. REFERENCE: WebFetch of https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK513288/ resolves to the StatPearls "Tetralogy of Fallot" chapter, which explicitly covers Tet spell pathophysiology (decreased SVR → increased R→L shunt) and management (knee-chest, oxygen, fluids, phenylephrine, beta-blockers, morphine). Directly on-topic and supports the keyed answer.

stem: A 68-year-old man is separating from cardiopulmonary bypass after three-vessel coronary artery bypass grafting. After an uneventful weaning, protamine is being administered to reverse heparin. Approximately two minutes into the infusion, the surgeon reports the right ventricle is acutely distended and poorly contractile. The pulmonary artery pressure has risen from 28/14 to 62/34 mmHg, while the systemic arterial pressure has fallen to 70/40 mmHg and central venous pressure is climbing. There is no rash or airway wheeze. What is the most appropriate immediate management?

choices.text: Continue the protamine to fully neutralize heparin and treat the hypotension with phenylephrine
choices.rationale: Incorrect: continuing the offending drug perpetuates the protamine-induced pulmonary vasoconstriction, and a pure alpha-agonist raises pulmonary vascular resistance further, worsening RV failure.

choices.text: Stop the protamine immediately, support the right ventricle, and consider re-establishing cardiopulmonary bypass if RV failure persists
choices.rationale: Correct: this is a catastrophic (type III) protamine reaction with acute pulmonary vasoconstriction and right heart failure, so the priorities are halting the protamine, providing inotropic/pulmonary-vasodilator support, and returning to bypass if the RV cannot recover.

choices.text: Administer a large IV bolus of additional heparin to reverse the protamine effect
choices.rationale: Incorrect: re-heparinization does not relieve the pulmonary vasoconstriction and reintroduces full anticoagulation in a patient with surgical hemostasis at risk.

choices.text: Give IV epinephrine and corticosteroids and rapidly transfuse to treat presumed anaphylaxis
choices.rationale: Incorrect: the absence of rash, urticaria, or bronchospasm with isolated pulmonary hypertension and RV failure points to pulmonary vasoconstriction rather than a type II anaphylactoid reaction, and aggressive volume loading would further distend the failing RV.

choices.text: Increase the protamine infusion rate to shorten the exposure and finish reversal quickly
choices.rationale: Incorrect: rapid protamine administration is itself a trigger for adverse reactions, and increasing the rate intensifies the pulmonary vasoconstrictive response.

explanation: Severe protamine reactions are classically grouped into type I (hypotension from rapid administration via histamine/NO and calcium-mediated vasodilation), type II (anaphylactoid/immunologic, with urticaria and bronchospasm), and type III (catastrophic pulmonary vasoconstriction, thought to be thromboxane- and complement-mediated). The type III reaction produces acute pulmonary hypertension, RV distension and failure, and systemic hypotension—exactly the picture here, without the cutaneous or airway signs of anaphylaxis. Management is to stop the protamine, support the RV with inotropes and pulmonary vasodilators, and re-establish cardiopulmonary bypass if RV failure is refractory.
subtopic: Cardiopulmonary bypass pharmacology: catastrophic (type III) protamine reaction and pulmonary vasoconstriction

verdict.reason: Clinical content is sound and unambiguous. The stem describes a textbook catastrophic (Type III, Horrow classification) protamine reaction: acute pulmonary hypertension (PA 28/14 to 62/34), RV distension/failure, rising CVP, systemic hypotension, ~2 min into infusion, with no cutaneous or airway signs to suggest anaphylaxis. Keyed answer B (stop protamine, support the RV with inotropes/pulmonary vasodilators, return to CPB if refractory) is the single best management and matches standard cardiac anesthesia teaching. All distractors are genuinely inferior: A (continue protamine + phenylephrine) perpetuates the trigger and phenylephrine raises PVR worsening RV failure; C (bolus heparin) does not relieve pulmonary vasoconstriction and reintroduces anticoagulation; D (treat as anaphylaxis with steroids + rapid transfusion) is wrong given absent cutaneous/airway signs, and aggressive volume loading distends the failing RV; E (increase protamine rate) intensifies the reaction. The Type I/II/III classification and thromboxane/complement mechanism are accurate and current. REFERENCE: WebFetch of https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK547753/ resolves to the StatPearls "Protamine" article, which explicitly covers heparin reversal, catastrophic pulmonary hypertension from thromboxane A2 (PVR up to 10x), prostacyclin (pulmonary vasodilator) treatment, and anaphylactoid vs hypotensive reactions. The citation is valid and directly on-topic. Both clinical and reference checks pass.

stem: A 74-year-old woman with severe symptomatic aortic stenosis (valve area 0.7 cm², mean gradient 48 mmHg) presents for an urgent open reduction and internal fixation of a hip fracture. She has preserved left ventricular ejection fraction and is in sinus rhythm. Shortly after a spinal anesthetic is dosed, her heart rate rises to 122/min with new ST-segment depression, and her blood pressure falls to 78/46 mmHg. Which combination of immediate interventions best matches the hemodynamic goals for this lesion?

choices.text: An IV phenylephrine bolus to restore systemic vascular resistance together with a beta-blocker or other rate-controlling measure to slow the heart rate
choices.rationale: Correct: in severe aortic stenosis the priorities are maintaining systemic vascular resistance (and thus coronary perfusion of the hypertrophied LV) and avoiding tachycardia, so raising SVR while slowing the rate directly addresses the hypotension and demand ischemia.

choices.text: An IV ephedrine bolus and an atropine dose to support blood pressure and chronotropy
choices.rationale: Incorrect: both agents increase heart rate, which shortens diastolic coronary filling and worsens subendocardial ischemia in the hypertrophied, pressure-overloaded ventricle.

choices.text: An IV nitroglycerin infusion to relieve the ST-segment changes by reducing myocardial oxygen demand
choices.rationale: Incorrect: nitroglycerin reduces preload and SVR, dropping the fixed-output ventricle's filling and coronary perfusion pressure and precipitating profound hypotension in aortic stenosis.

choices.text: A rapid 2-liter crystalloid bolus alone, with no vasopressor, to correct presumed hypovolemia
choices.rationale: Incorrect: although preload should be maintained, volume alone neither restores the lost SVR nor controls the tachycardia driving the ischemia, leaving the core problem untreated.

choices.text: An IV dobutamine infusion to augment contractility and overcome the fixed obstruction
choices.rationale: Incorrect: dobutamine increases heart rate and lowers SVR, both detrimental in aortic stenosis, and inotropy cannot overcome a fixed mechanical obstruction.

explanation: The hypertrophied left ventricle in severe aortic stenosis is preload-dependent, depends on sinus rhythm and an adequate diastolic interval for filling, and relies on a high systemic (and therefore coronary perfusion) pressure to perfuse the thickened myocardium against high intracavitary pressures. Sympathetic blockade from a spinal can drop SVR and trigger a reflex tachycardia, producing hypotension and demand ischemia. The correct response restores SVR with an alpha-agonist such as phenylephrine and controls the heart rate, while avoiding tachycardia-inducing or vasodilating agents that further compromise coronary perfusion of the pressure-overloaded ventricle.
subtopic: Valvular heart disease: hemodynamic management of severe aortic stenosis for noncardiac surgery
reference_title: Aortic Stenosis: Hemodynamic Management, Comorbidities, and Treatment (OpenAnesthesia)

verdict.reason: CLINICAL: Sound and unambiguous. The keyed answer (phenylephrine to restore SVR + rate control) is the single best choice and reflects standard AS hemodynamic goals: maintain afterload/SVR for coronary perfusion of the hypertrophied LV, avoid tachycardia to preserve diastolic filling/coronary perfusion time, maintain preload and sinus rhythm. The post-spinal picture (sympathectomy-induced SVR drop with reflex tachycardia, ST depression, hypotension) is a textbook decompensation. All four distractors are genuinely inferior: ephedrine+atropine both raise HR; nitroglycerin drops preload/SVR (dangerous in AS); crystalloid alone fails to restore SVR or control rate; dobutamine raises HR, lowers SVR, and cannot overcome a fixed obstruction. Rationales and explanation are accurate and current. One minor adversarial nuance — pairing phenylephrine with a beta-blocker in a hypotensive patient could be hazardous if given before pressure is restored — but the option is worded as a combination and phenylephrine's reflex bradycardia is the dominant standard teaching; this does not create a wrong key or two defensible answers. Citation is valid and relevant.

stem: A 58-year-old woman undergoes a posterior cervical laminectomy in the sitting (beach-chair) position under general endotracheal anesthesia. Forty minutes into the case, while the surgeon is working in the surgical field, the capnograph EtCO2 abruptly falls from 36 mmHg to 14 mmHg over about 30 seconds. Simultaneously the SpO2 drifts from 99% to 92%, the heart rate rises to 118 bpm, and the systolic blood pressure drops from 118 to 76 mmHg. Breath sounds reveal a new harsh precordial 'mill-wheel' murmur. Peak inspiratory pressures are unchanged and the endotracheal tube position is confirmed at the teeth. Which of the following best explains the sudden decrease in end-tidal CO2?

choices.text: Acute bronchospasm obstructing expiratory gas flow
choices.rationale: Bronchospasm raises peak inspiratory pressures and produces an upsloping ('shark-fin') expiratory limb rather than an abrupt fall in EtCO2 with preserved airway pressures.

choices.text: Inadvertent endobronchial migration of the endotracheal tube
choices.rationale: Endobronchial intubation increases peak inspiratory pressure and causes unilateral breath sounds and hypoxemia, not a precipitous EtCO2 drop with a mill-wheel murmur and hypotension.

choices.text: Venous air embolism causing a sudden fall in pulmonary blood flow and increased alveolar dead space
choices.rationale: Entrained air in the right heart and pulmonary arteries obstructs pulmonary perfusion, raising the dead-space fraction so less CO2 reaches the alveoli, producing the abrupt EtCO2 drop, hypotension, tachycardia, and the classic mill-wheel murmur seen in the seated position.

choices.text: Hypoventilation from a leak in the breathing circuit
choices.rationale: A circuit leak lowers delivered tidal volume and tends to raise (not lower) EtCO2 from CO2 retention while also dropping airway pressures and triggering a low-volume alarm, and it does not produce a cardiac murmur or hemodynamic collapse.

choices.text: Rising malignant hyperthermia-associated hypermetabolism
choices.rationale: Malignant hyperthermia produces a progressive RISE in EtCO2 from increased CO2 production along with tachycardia and rigidity, the opposite of the abrupt fall described here.

explanation: Capnography is the most sensitive routine monitor for venous air embolism, which is a recognized hazard of the sitting/beach-chair position because the surgical field is above the heart. Entrained air lodges in the right ventricular outflow tract and pulmonary arteries, acutely reducing pulmonary blood flow; the resulting increase in alveolar dead space means inspired air-filled alveoli are ventilated but not perfused, so end-tidal CO2 drops sharply. The accompanying hypotension, tachycardia, hypoxemia, and 'mill-wheel' murmur complete the picture. Management includes flooding the field, lowering the head, aspirating air through a central line, and supporting hemodynamics.

All four distractors are genuinely inferior and their rationales are correct: bronchospasm and endobronchial migration both RAISE peak inspiratory pressures (stated unchanged) and are explicitly excluded; a circuit leak would trigger a low-volume/low-pressure alarm and not produce a murmur or collapse; malignant hyperthermia causes a progressive RISE in EtCO2 (the opposite). stem: A 72-year-old man with a BMI of 34 kg/m2 and obstructive sleep apnea undergoes an open ventral hernia repair under general anesthesia with rocuronium for relaxation. At the end of the case the surgeon requests no further muscle relaxant. You quantitatively measure a train-of-four ratio of 0.6 at the adductor pollicis using acceleromyography, and the patient is beginning to breathe spontaneously. Before you proceed with reversal and extubation, which finding most reliably confirms that recovery from neuromuscular blockade is adequate for safe extubation?

choices.text: A quantitatively measured train-of-four ratio of at least 0.9 at the adductor pollicis
choices.rationale: A TOF ratio of 0.9 or greater measured by an objective (quantitative) monitor is the validated threshold that excludes clinically significant residual blockade and pharyngeal/upper-airway dysfunction, making it the most reliable confirmation of adequate recovery.

choices.text: Absence of visible or tactile fade on a qualitative train-of-four with a peripheral nerve stimulator
choices.rationale: Subjective evaluation cannot detect fade once the TOF ratio exceeds roughly 0.4, so the absence of perceived fade can occur with a true ratio as low as 0.4 and badly underestimates residual blockade.

choices.text: A sustained head lift for 5 seconds on command
choices.rationale: Sustained head lift is a useful clinical sign but corresponds to a TOF ratio of only about 0.5-0.6 and can be present despite clinically important residual weakness, so it does not confirm full recovery.

choices.text: A vital capacity of 15 mL/kg with an adequate tidal volume
choices.rationale: Tidal volume and vital capacity are relatively insensitive to partial paralysis and can appear normal at TOF ratios well below 0.9, missing residual blockade that still impairs airway protection.

choices.text: Five strong twitches with no fade on a single double-burst stimulation by feel
choices.rationale: Tactile double-burst assessment improves fade detection over TOF but is still subjective and unreliable above a ratio of about 0.6, so it cannot positively confirm a ratio of 0.9.

explanation: Residual neuromuscular blockade is defined as a train-of-four ratio below 0.9 at the adductor pollicis and is associated with upper-airway obstruction, impaired hypoxic ventilatory drive, aspiration, and postoperative pulmonary complications. Clinical tests (head lift, vital capacity) and qualitative nerve-stimulator assessments (visual/tactile TOF or double-burst) all become unable to detect fade once the ratio rises above roughly 0.4-0.6, so they cannot confirm full recovery. Only objective quantitative monitoring documenting a TOF ratio of at least 0.9 reliably establishes adequate recovery; with a measured ratio of 0.6 this patient still requires reversal before extubation.
subtopic: Neuromuscular monitoring (train-of-four ratio and residual blockade)

verdict.reason: CLINICAL: Keyed answer A (quantitative TOF ratio >=0.9 at adductor pollicis) is the single best answer and matches the 2023 ASA guideline standard for excluding clinically significant residual neuromuscular blockade. All distractors are genuinely inferior with accurate rationales: B (qualitative TOF fade undetectable above ~0.4), C (head lift only corresponds to TOF ~0.5-0.6), D (vital capacity/tidal volume insensitive to partial paralysis), E (tactile double-burst unreliable above ~0.6). Stem is internally consistent (measured TOF 0.6 needs reversal) and unambiguous; OSA/obesity context appropriately heightens the relevance of residual blockade. The acceleromyography vs mechanomyography normalization nuance does not undermine the answer. Explanation is accurate and current. REFERENCE: WebFetch of https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK538301/ resolves to StatPearls 'Neuromuscular Blockade,' which explicitly states a TOFR <0.9 indicates residual blockade requiring reversal, recommends quantitative over qualitative monitoring per 2023 ASA guidelines, and advises confirming TOF ratio >=0.9 before extubation. Citation is accurate, on-topic, and supports the keyed answer.

stem: A 24-year-old healthy woman (ASA II) receives general endotracheal anesthesia for an elective laparoscopic cholecystectomy in a 20°C operating room. A nasopharyngeal temperature probe is placed after induction. Despite a forced-air warming blanket being applied to the lower body, her core temperature is recorded as 36.8°C at induction, 35.6°C at 30 minutes, and 35.4°C at 50 minutes. The anesthesia care team is surprised by how quickly the temperature fell in the first half hour. Which mechanism is most responsible for the rapid 1-1.5°C decline in core temperature seen during the first hour after induction?

choices.text: Evaporative water loss from the open surgical incision and exposed viscera
choices.rationale: Evaporative loss contributes only a small fraction of total perioperative heat loss and is minimal during a laparoscopic case with a closed abdomen, so it cannot account for the rapid early drop.

choices.text: Convective heat loss from cold operating-room airflow over exposed skin
choices.rationale: Convection is a meaningful contributor but drives the slower second-phase linear cooling that occurs over hours, not the steep first-hour fall in core temperature.

choices.text: Radiative heat transfer from the patient to the cold walls and equipment of the operating room
choices.rationale: Radiation is the single largest route of true heat loss to the environment, but it produces gradual cooling rather than the abrupt early core-temperature decrease that occurs before substantial net heat is lost.

choices.text: Internal redistribution of body heat from the warm core to the cooler periphery after anesthesia-induced vasodilation
choices.rationale: Anesthetics inhibit tonic thermoregulatory vasoconstriction, so warm core blood flows to vasodilated peripheral tissues and the core temperature falls 1-1.5°C in the first hour even though little net body heat has yet been lost to the room.

choices.text: Suppression of metabolic heat production by volatile anesthetic agents
choices.rationale: General anesthesia lowers metabolic heat production by only about 15-30%, an effect far too small and gradual to explain a 1-1.5°C core drop within the first hour.

explanation: Perioperative hypothermia classically follows three phases. The steep first-hour decline of 1-1.5°C is dominated by internal redistribution: general (and neuraxial) anesthesia abolishes the tonic vasoconstriction that normally confines warm blood to the core, so heat flows down a temperature gradient into the cooler, newly vasodilated peripheral compartment. This is a shift of existing body heat rather than true loss to the environment, which is why it occurs rapidly and is difficult to prevent with intraoperative warming alone. Pre-warming the periphery before induction narrows the core-to-periphery gradient and is the most effective strategy against redistribution hypothermia; environmental losses by radiation and convection drive the slower second linear phase.
subtopic: Thermoregulation and temperature monitoring (redistribution hypothermia)

verdict.reason: CLINICAL: Keyed answer (index 3, redistribution of body heat from core to vasodilated periphery) is the single best and unambiguous answer. The classic Sessler three-phase model attributes the rapid 1-1.5C first-hour core decline to redistribution after anesthesia abolishes tonic thermoregulatory vasoconstriction — exactly what the stem describes. All four distractors are genuinely inferior and their rationales are accurate: evaporative loss is minimal in a closed laparoscopic abdomen; convection and radiation drive the slower second/linear phase; metabolic suppression (~15-30%) is too small/gradual. The stem is internally consistent (laparoscopic case, 20C OR, nasopharyngeal core probe, lower-body forced-air warmer still failing to prevent the drop — which correctly illustrates that redistribution resists intraoperative warming) and the temperature trajectory (36.8 to 35.6 to 35.4) matches the steep-then-plateau pattern. The explanation, including pre-warming the periphery as the most effective countermeasure, is current and correct. Minor non-issue: calling radiation 'the single largest route' of environmental loss is a commonly taught point and does not create a competing best answer since the stem asks about the rapid first-hour drop. REFERENCE: WebFetch of the OpenAnesthesia URL resolves (not 404) and is squarely on-topic, with near-verbatim support: 'core temperature decreases rapidly by 1-1.5C due to redistribution from the core to peripheral tissues from anesthesia-induced vasodilation.' Both checks pass.

stem: An 82-year-old woman with well-controlled hypertension and mild osteoarthritis is scheduled for elective laparoscopic cholecystectomy under general anesthesia. She lives independently, scores 30/30 on a preoperative Mini-Mental State Examination, and takes only lisinopril. She is anxious in the holding area and asks for 'something to calm her nerves.' The anesthesiologist supervising the case is focused on minimizing this patient's risk of postoperative delirium. Which of the following preoperative or intraoperative choices is MOST consistent with that goal?

choices.text: Administer 2 mg of midazolam IV for anxiolysis before transport to the operating room
choices.rationale: Benzodiazepines are among the medications most strongly associated with postoperative delirium in older adults and are specifically recommended to be limited or avoided for routine premedication in this population.

choices.text: Add a dexmedetomidine infusion as part of the anesthetic plan rather than relying on a benzodiazepine for sedation
choices.rationale: Dexmedetomidine is the intervention with the best supporting evidence for reducing delirium and is preferred over benzodiazepine-based sedation in patients at risk for postoperative delirium.

choices.text: Give 0.4 mg of scopolamine transdermally preoperatively to reduce postoperative nausea and provide sedation
choices.rationale: Scopolamine is a centrally acting anticholinergic that crosses the blood-brain barrier and is a recognized precipitant of delirium in elderly patients, so it should be avoided.

choices.text: Administer diphenhydramine 25 mg IV preoperatively as a mild sedative-anxiolytic
choices.rationale: Diphenhydramine has potent central anticholinergic activity and is one of the inappropriate medications most likely to provoke delirium in older adults.

choices.text: Plan a deeper-than-usual anesthetic depth to ensure she has no intraoperative awareness, which is a known delirium trigger
choices.rationale: Excessively deep anesthesia (and the larger drug doses it requires) is associated with delirium rather than protective against it, and routinely targeting a deeper plane is not an evidence-based delirium-prevention strategy.

explanation: Postoperative delirium prevention in older adults centers on avoiding deliriogenic drugs and supporting the brain. Benzodiazepines and centrally acting anticholinergics (scopolamine, diphenhydramine) are among the medications most consistently linked to delirium and should be limited. Dexmedetomidine has the strongest evidence base as a substitute sedative, and avoiding both excessive and inadequate anesthetic depth (rather than deliberately going deep) is recommended.
subtopic: Postoperative delirium prevention and deliriogenic medications
reference_title: Postoperative Delirium in Aging Patients - OpenAnesthesia

verdict.reason: CLINICAL: The keyed answer (B, dexmedetomidine over benzodiazepine sedation) is the single best answer. All four distractors are genuinely inferior and each rationale is accurate: midazolam (A) and the central anticholinergics scopolamine (C) and diphenhydramine (D) are all Beers-Criteria deliriogenic agents to avoid; deliberately deep anesthesia (E) is associated with—not protective against—POD, and the "avoid awareness" framing is an appropriate distractor. The explanation is accurate and current. The dexmedetomidine choice text is appropriately hedged ("best supporting evidence... preferred over benzodiazepine-based sedation"), so the mixed signal from some recent trials does not create real ambiguity relative to four clearly wrong options. REFERENCE: WebFetch of https://www.openanesthesia.org/keywords/postoperative-delirium-in-aging-patients/ resolved successfully; the page is titled "Postoperative Delirium in Aging Patients" and explicitly covers benzodiazepines, scopolamine/anticholinergics (Beers Criteria), recommends "Use dexmedetomidine," and discusses anesthetic depth—directly on-topic and corroborating every teaching point. Both clinical and reference are sound.

stem: A 78-year-old man (72 kg, BMI 24) with a remote, asymptomatic myocardial infarction five years ago, well-controlled type 2 diabetes, and an estimated GFR of 55 mL/min presents for total knee arthroplasty under general anesthesia. The trainee proposes to induce with the same weight-based propofol bolus they routinely use for a healthy 30-year-old (2.5 mg/kg) and to run sevoflurane at the standard adult MAC. Considering the pharmacologic changes of aging, which adjustment is MOST appropriate?

choices.text: Use the full 2.5 mg/kg propofol bolus but reduce the maintenance infusion rate only
choices.rationale: Older patients have both increased pharmacodynamic sensitivity to the induction bolus and slower clearance, so the bolus itself must be reduced, not just the infusion.

choices.text: Reduce the propofol induction dose to roughly 1-1.75 mg/kg and target a lower sevoflurane concentration because MAC decreases with age
choices.rationale: Older adults are about 30% more sensitive to propofol, warranting a 40-50% bolus reduction, and MAC falls roughly 6% per decade after age 40, so both agents should be dosed lower.

choices.text: Keep both propofol and sevoflurane at standard adult doses because anesthetic requirements are unchanged until the ninth decade
choices.rationale: Anesthetic requirements decline progressively from middle age onward, not only after 90, so standard doses risk overdose and prolonged emergence.

choices.text: Increase the sevoflurane concentration above standard adult MAC because aging raises minimum alveolar concentration
choices.rationale: MAC decreases rather than increases with age, so raising the concentration would deepen anesthesia inappropriately and prolong recovery.

choices.text: Reduce the propofol bolus but increase the maintenance infusion to compensate for the larger volume of distribution in the elderly
choices.rationale: Aging is associated with a smaller central volume of distribution and reduced clearance, so the maintenance infusion should be decreased, not increased.

explanation: Aging produces a smaller central volume of distribution, reduced clearance, and increased end-organ sensitivity, so both intravenous and inhaled anesthetic doses should be reduced. Propofol bolus should be cut by roughly 40-50% (to about 1-1.75 mg/kg) because elderly patients are about 30% more pharmacodynamically sensitive, and MAC declines approximately 6% per decade beyond age 40. Failing to make these adjustments predisposes to hypotension, delayed emergence, and postoperative cognitive complications.
subtopic: Geriatric pharmacology: age-related dosing of IV and inhaled anesthetics
reference_title: Pharmacological Changes with Aging: Inhalational and Intravenous Anesthetics - OpenAnesthesia

verdict.reason:
CLINICAL: Keyed answer B (reduce propofol to ~1-1.75 mg/kg and target lower sevoflurane because MAC falls with age) is unambiguously the single best answer and reflects standard geriatric pharmacology: ~30% increased pharmacodynamic sensitivity to propofol warranting a 40-50% bolus reduction, and MAC decreasing ~6% per decade. All four distractors are genuinely inferior with internally consistent rationales: A (reduce infusion only, keep full bolus) is wrong because the bolus itself must be cut; C (requirements unchanged until 9th decade) is factually false; D (raise MAC with age) inverts the correct direction; E (increase infusion for a 'larger' Vd) is contradicted by the smaller central Vd and reduced clearance of aging. One minor nuance: the explanation's 'smaller central volume of distribution' reflects conventional teaching (and is consistent with the cited source); the Schnider model technically holds V1 age-invariant, but this does not undermine the key or create ambiguity. The 6%/decade figure is standard teaching and matches the reference.

REFERENCE: WebFetch of the OpenAnesthesia URL resolves (HTTP 200) and is directly on-topic ('Pharmacological Changes with Aging: Inhalational and Intravenous Anesthetics'). It explicitly states propofol induction should be reduced 40-50% to 1-1.75 mg/kg, that geriatric patients are 30% more sensitive to propofol, and that MAC decreases ~6% per decade — corroborating the keyed answer and explanation. No reference problem.

Both clinical and reference are sound; keep.

stem: An 84-year-old woman (50 kg) undergoes a 3-hour open hemicolectomy under general anesthesia in an operating room kept at 20°C. No active warming was used during induction or the first hour. At the end of the case her bladder (core) temperature is 34.8°C. She is visibly shivering in the PACU, her INR has risen, and the surgeon notes more diffuse oozing than expected. Regarding the mechanism and management of this patient's hypothermia, which statement is MOST accurate?

choices.text: The largest single drop in core temperature occurred during the first hour and was driven primarily by core-to-peripheral redistribution of heat after anesthetic-induced vasodilation
choices.rationale: The first phase of intraoperative hypothermia is a rapid 1-1.5°C fall within the first hour caused by redistribution of heat from the warm core to vasodilated peripheral tissues, which is the dominant early mechanism.

choices.text: Her coagulopathy is unrelated to temperature, since clotting enzyme kinetics and platelet function are unaffected until core temperature falls below 30°C
choices.rationale: Even mild hypothermia (around 34-35°C) impairs platelet function and slows enzymatic coagulation, directly contributing to her oozing and rising INR.

choices.text: Pre-warming the skin before induction would have been ineffective because peripheral tissue temperature has no influence on redistribution hypothermia
choices.rationale: Pre-warming raises peripheral tissue temperature and narrows the core-to-peripheral gradient, which is the single most effective way to blunt redistribution hypothermia, so it would have helped.

choices.text: General anesthesia raises the shivering and vasoconstriction thresholds, which is why her thermoregulatory defenses failed intraoperatively
choices.rationale: General anesthetics lower (not raise) the thresholds for vasoconstriction and shivering, widening the interthreshold range and impairing thermoregulatory defenses.

choices.text: Active forced-air warming should be withheld now because rewarming a shivering elderly patient sharply increases the risk of malignant hyperthermia
choices.rationale: Forced-air warming is the appropriate treatment for postoperative hypothermia and shivering and has no relationship to malignant hyperthermia, which is a pharmacogenetic trigger response.

explanation: Intraoperative hypothermia classically unfolds in three phases: a rapid first-hour redistribution of heat from core to periphery after anesthetic-induced vasodilation (the largest early drop), a slower linear decline, and finally a plateau. General anesthetics impair thermoregulation by lowering the vasoconstriction and shivering thresholds, and elderly patients have blunted defenses and high surface-area-to-mass ratios. Even mild hypothermia impairs coagulation and platelet function, so prevention via pre-warming and intraoperative active warming, plus forced-air rewarming postoperatively, is the correct approach.
subtopic: Perioperative thermoregulation and inadvertent hypothermia
reference_title: Hypothermia: prevention and management in adults having surgery - NCBI Bookshelf

verdict.reason: CLINICAL: Keyed answer (choice 0) is the single best answer and is accurate — Sessler's three-phase model has the first-hour core-to-peripheral redistribution after anesthetic-induced vasodilation as the largest single drop (~1-1.5°C, dominant early mechanism). All four distractors contain a clearly false primary assertion: (1) mild hypothermia DOES impair platelets/enzymatic coagulation at 34-35°C, not only below 30°C; (2) pre-warming raises peripheral temperature and narrows the gradient, so it IS effective; (3) general anesthetics LOWER (not raise) vasoconstriction/shivering thresholds; (4) forced-air rewarming has no link to malignant hyperthermia and is the correct treatment. Each distractor's rationale correctly states the true physiology, confirming intent. The explanation (three-phase course, threshold lowering, blunted elderly defenses, mild-hypothermia coagulopathy, pre-warming + active warming + forced-air rewarming) is accurate and current. No ambiguity; the only minor nuance (redistribution somewhat attenuated in a thin elderly patient) does not displace it as the best answer. REFERENCE: WebFetch of https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK554181/ resolves to NICE CG65 'Hypothermia: prevention and management in adults having surgery,' which matches reference_title and is squarely on-topic (defines hypothermia <36°C, covers redistribution/vasodilation mechanism, pre/intra/postoperative phases, forced-air warming, fluid warming). On-topic and valid.

stem: A 27-year-old man arrives after a single stab wound to the left parasternal chest. He is anxious and diaphoretic. Vitals: HR 128, BP 84/68 (narrow pulse pressure), and the jugular veins are distended despite the hypotension; heart sounds are muffled. A FAST exam shows a pericardial effusion, and BP falls further during a brief trial of head-up positioning. The trauma surgeon plans an emergent subxiphoid pericardial window/sternotomy and asks the anesthesia team to prepare for induction. Which induction approach is MOST appropriate for this patient's physiology?

choices.text: Perform a deliberately slow, deep inhalational induction with sevoflurane to blunt sympathetic surges before laryngoscopy
choices.rationale: A slow deep volatile induction produces vasodilation and myocardial depression in a preload-dependent, sympathetically-maxed patient and can precipitate collapse before the chest is open.

choices.text: Give a generous dose of propofol with early aggressive positive-pressure ventilation and high PEEP to optimize oxygenation before the incision
choices.rationale: Propofol-induced vasodilation/myocardial depression and high intrathoracic pressure from PPV/PEEP both reduce venous return to an already compressed heart, risking cardiac arrest.

choices.text: Pretreat with a beta-blocker to control the heart rate of 128 before inducing, since the tachycardia is increasing myocardial oxygen demand
choices.rationale: The tachycardia is a compensatory mechanism maintaining cardiac output in a fixed-stroke-volume state, and blunting it removes a critical compensation and can precipitate decompensation.

choices.text: Maintain spontaneous ventilation and a full intravascular volume, keep the patient relatively tachycardic, and have the surgeon prepped and draped before a cautious induction — because positive-pressure ventilation and vasodilation can precipitate cardiovascular collapse
choices.rationale: Correct — tamponade is a fixed low-stroke-volume state dependent on preload, heart rate, and contractility, and positive-pressure ventilation plus anesthetic-induced vasodilation can abruptly drop venous return and cause arrest, so the surgeon should be ready to decompress immediately at induction.

choices.text: Aggressively lower the blood pressure with nitroglycerin to reduce wall stress and 'unload' the heart before surgical decompression
choices.rationale: Vasodilator-induced preload and afterload reduction is dangerous in tamponade, where maintaining filling pressure and systemic vascular tone is essential until the pericardium is drained.

explanation: Penetrating cardiac injury with pericardial tamponade produces Beck's triad (hypotension, distended neck veins, muffled heart sounds) and a narrow pulse pressure because the heart is compressed and stroke volume is fixed and low. Cardiac output then depends on preload, heart rate, and sympathetic tone, so the safe anesthetic plan keeps the patient 'full, fast, and tight' — preserve volume, allow compensatory tachycardia, support contractility/vasoconstriction, and ideally maintain spontaneous ventilation. Because induction agents cause vasodilation and positive-pressure ventilation further reduces venous return, the surgeon should be scrubbed, prepped, and ready to open the chest the moment anesthesia is induced.
subtopic: Penetrating cardiac injury with pericardial tamponade — Beck's triad and induction hemodynamics

verdict.reason:
CLINICAL (sound): The stem is a textbook penetrating cardiac injury with pericardial tamponade — Beck's triad (hypotension, JVD, muffled heart sounds), narrow pulse pressure, compensatory tachycardia, FAST effusion, and BP falling with head-up positioning (preload drop). The keyed answer (index 3) — maintain spontaneous ventilation, keep volume full and patient relatively tachycardic, support tone, and have the surgeon prepped/draped before a cautious induction because PPV and anesthetic vasodilation can precipitate collapse — is the single best answer and reflects the standard "full, fast, and tight" tamponade approach with surgeon ready to decompress at induction. All four distractors are genuinely inferior and correctly explained: slow deep sevoflurane (vasodilation/myocardial depression), propofol + aggressive PPV/high PEEP (reduces venous return → arrest), beta-blockade (removes compensatory tachycardia in a fixed-stroke-volume state), and nitroglycerin (vasodilator contraindicated in preload-dependent tamponade). The explanation and per-choice rationales are accurate and current. Answer is unambiguous.

REFERENCE (broken/mismatched): The citation is wrong. reference_url https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK430685/ resolves to the StatPearls *collection landing/index page* (NBK430685 = "StatPearls [Internet]" homepage/table of contents), NOT an article on acute cardiac tamponade. WebFetch returned only the StatPearls topic index with no tamponade article body. WebSearch confirms the actual StatPearls tamponade articles are different IDs: "Acute Cardiac Tamponade" = NBK534806 and "Cardiac Tamponade" = NBK431090. So the reference_title ("Acute Cardiac Tamponade, StatPearls") is mismatched to the URL — the link does not land on the cited content. This is a citation/reference defect requiring correction to NBK534806 (or NBK431090), even though the medicine is correct.

stem: A 52-year-old woman undergoes total thyroidectomy for a large multinodular goiter. After an uneventful tracheal extubation, she develops mild stridor and a hoarse, breathy voice but maintains adequate oxygenation. Intraoperative neuromonitoring had shown a loss of signal on one side. Injury to which nerve best explains this unilateral presentation?

choices.text: Recurrent laryngeal nerve
choices.rationale: Correct: unilateral recurrent laryngeal nerve injury paralyzes the ipsilateral vocal cord, producing hoarseness and a breathy voice, and is the classic complication monitored during thyroidectomy.

choices.text: External branch of the superior laryngeal nerve
choices.rationale: Incorrect: superior laryngeal nerve (external branch) injury weakens the cricothyroid and causes voice fatigue and loss of high pitch, not the breathy hoarseness of cord paralysis.

choices.text: Glossopharyngeal nerve
choices.rationale: Incorrect: the glossopharyngeal nerve mediates posterior tongue sensation and pharyngeal function and is not at risk in thyroid surgery, nor does it move the cords.

choices.text: Hypoglossal nerve
choices.rationale: Incorrect: hypoglossal injury produces tongue deviation and dysarthria, not vocal cord paralysis.

choices.text: Phrenic nerve
choices.rationale: Incorrect: phrenic injury causes hemidiaphragm paralysis and dyspnea, not hoarseness from a paralyzed cord.

explanation: The recurrent laryngeal nerve supplies all intrinsic laryngeal muscles except the cricothyroid, so a unilateral injury leaves the affected cord in a paramedian position, producing hoarseness and a breathy voice while usually preserving the airway. Bilateral RLN injury is the feared emergency, causing both cords to sit near the midline with severe stridor and possible complete airway obstruction requiring reintubation. The external branch of the superior laryngeal nerve, by contrast, affects voice pitch and projection. Intraoperative loss of signal on neuromonitoring helps predict postoperative cord dysfunction.
subtopic: Recurrent laryngeal nerve injury after thyroid surgery
reference_title: Recurrent Laryngeal Nerve Injury - StatPearls - NCBI Bookshelf

verdict.reason: CLINICAL (sound): The keyed answer (recurrent laryngeal nerve, index 0) is the single best answer. The stem describes classic unilateral RLN injury after thyroidectomy: paramedian cord paralysis producing hoarse breathy voice with preserved oxygenation, plus intraoperative loss of signal on neuromonitoring. All distractors are genuinely inferior: external branch of SLN affects cricothyroid (pitch/projection, not breathy paralysis); glossopharyngeal, hypoglossal, and phrenic nerves do not move the vocal cords and (glossopharyngeal/hypoglossal/phrenic) are not the monitored thyroidectomy nerve. The explanation is accurate and current — RLN supplies all intrinsic laryngeal muscles except cricothyroid, and bilateral RLN injury (both cords near midline, severe stridor/obstruction) is correctly identified as the feared emergency. Minor nuance: 'mild stridor' is slightly atypical for truly isolated unilateral RLN injury (usually breathy hoarseness with minimal stridor), but this is plausible and creates no ambiguity. REFERENCE (broken/mismatched): reference_url https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK560853/ resolves but the page is titled 'Orientation to the Medical Simulation Environment,' a prebriefing/simulation-education article with no content on the recurrent laryngeal nerve, vocal cords, or thyroid surgery. The claimed reference_title 'Anatomy, Head and Neck, Recurrent Laryngeal Nerve - StatPearls' does not match the actual page (verified via two WebFetch calls). This is a fabricated/mismatched citation — the NBK ID does not correspond to the cited article. Per the classification rule, clinical content is sound so this is a reference problem requiring keep=false.
